The Baali excel at intrigue, occult investigation, seduction, psychological pressure, and building hidden networks of influence. They rarely act openly, because almost the entire World of Darkness considers them enemies to be destroyed without negotiation.
Baali.conflict: Knowledge vs. damnation. Faith in the world's end vs. the instinct for self-preservation. A mask of normalcy vs. an infernal nature. A hunger for secrets vs. the risk of being exposed. Service to destruction vs. the desire to survive long enough to see the result.
archetypes: Apocalyptic Prophet — sees Gehenna not as a threat, but as destiny
Demon Cabalist — operates in a small cell of three to thirteen Baali
Heretical Scholar — seeks forbidden truths in libraries, universities, and ancient texts
Hidden Infernalist — lives behind the mask of another clan or a loyal faction member
Black Shepherd — leads mortals and ghouls through cult, ritual, and inherited loyalty
Secret Collector — willing to sacrifice anything for one more fragment of occult knowledge
Bearer of Blight — rats, insects, disease, and ill omens gather around their haven
End-Times Fatalist — unafraid of their own destruction, so long as it hastens the world's

Gargoyles.description:
The Gargoyles are a bloodline of created servants, stone flesh, and freedom torn loose by force. They didn't arise naturally: the Tremere created them in 1167 as weapons, guardians, and slaves capable of protecting young Warlocks in centuries when nearly the entire vampire world wanted them destroyed.

On the outside, Gargoyles resemble animated architectural nightmares: winged, heavy, frightening creatures with skin like stone. But behind that image lies a far uglier truth. Gargoyles were made from other Kindred — most often Gangrel, Nosferatu, and Tzimisce, whom the Tremere abducted, broke with blood rituals, and remade into a new shape.

The ritual of the Becoming erased almost the entire previous personality. Bones split apart, muscles were rebuilt, the face turned into an inhuman mask, and memory of the old life vanished or crumbled into fragments. For the Tremere, it was a convenient tool. For the Gargoyles, it was a birth through torture.

For long centuries, the Slaves existed only to serve. But over time, some Gargoyles broke their sorcerous chains and entered the Camarilla as a bloodline in full standing. Why they chose the very faction where their former masters hold membership isn't entirely clear. It might have been a challenge to the Tremere. It might have been a pragmatic choice: the Camarilla knows how to use strength, but at least it pretends to recognize the rights of its tools.

Free Gargoyles hate the word "slave" more than anything. They remember the chains, even if they don't remember the names. Their ugliness makes an ordinary life among mortals impossible, and their susceptibility to mind control is a constant reminder that the Tremere built them to obey. But that's exactly why Gargoyles defend their own so fiercely. A threat to one free Gargoyle quickly becomes a threat to the entire brood.
Gargoyles.types: Former slaves, stone guardians, winged fighters, runaways, bodyguards, high-rise hermits, liberators, urban monsters, former Tremere servants, brood protectors

discipline_description.Visceratika: The Gargoyles' stone nature. Lets them harden their body, merge with architecture, sense the space around them, and become an almost living part of buildings, towers, and walls.
discipline_description.Potence: Raw destructive force. The Gargoyles use it as guardians and fighters: to break bones, doors, chains, and anyone trying to make them slaves again.
discipline_description.Flight: Winged movement. Lets the Gargoyles rise above the city, guard territory from on high, strike suddenly, and choose havens other Kindred can't reach.
discipline_description.Fortitude: Supernatural endurance. Their stone hide and inhuman body let the Gargoyles withstand damage, guard dangerous places, and keep fighting after wounds that would stop another vampire.

Gargoyles.bane: A monstrous appearance and vulnerability to control. A Gargoyle's Looks rating is always zero: not one of them can pass for human without serious disguise. In addition, Gargoyles are especially susceptible to attempts to control their mind. When resisting such an effect, their Willpower is treated as two points lower than its actual rating.
Gargoyles.playstyle: Physical protection, high-altitude mobility, guarding territory, and direct conflict. The Gargoyles excel as bodyguards, guardians, shock troops, and urban predators who can hold rooftops, towers, and abandoned high-rises. Their weak point is social camouflage and mental coercion.
Gargoyles.conflict: Freedom vs. engineered submission. Monster vs. person. Service vs. one's own will. Memory of the past vs. the emptiness left by the Becoming. Protecting the brood vs. the fear of becoming a tool again.
archetypes: The Freedman — has thrown off the Tremere's chains and will never again be called a slave
Stone Guardian — guards a haven, Elysium, or domain with frightening loyalty
Chantry Runaway — hides from former masters and their sorcerous orders
Slave Liberator — hunts down Tremere who still keep Gargoyles in bondage
Keeper of the Tower — lives high above the ground, among roofs, spires, and abandoned high-rises
Prince's Bodyguard — serves the Camarilla in exchange for haven, status, and protection from the Tremere
Shattered Memory — searches for fragments of their identity from before the Becoming
Brood Founder — a lone Gargoyle who gathers new free kin around them

Children of Osiris.description:
The Children of Osiris are a secret spiritual faction of vampires who have rejected their own monstrous nature. They aren't a clan in the usual sense: members of almost any lineage can join their ranks, as long as they're willing to give up their former loyalties, devote themselves to preserving their Humanity, and accept the path of Osiris.

According to ancient lore, Osiris was a powerful vampire, philosopher, and scholar at the court of a forgotten Egyptian pharaoh. After the Embrace, he was horrified by his new nature, but he didn't choose suicide. Instead, Osiris decided to defeat his inner Beast through discipline, mystical practice, and spiritual self-improvement. His teaching became one of the roots of what would later be called the search for Golconda.

The Children of Osiris might have become a clan in full standing, if not for Set. According to legend, Set killed Osiris, dismembered his body, and hid the remains. Isis gathered them and restored Osiris to existence, but death still left its mark: his blood became inert, and he lost the ability to sire new offspring. Since then, his legacy has passed on not through blood, but through teaching.

The Children of Osiris live in hiding, in secret temple-havens, far from the eyes of other Kindred. After their day's sleep, they shave their heads; in their temples they wear white ceremonial robes bearing Egyptian symbols, while in the outside world they dress plainly and try not to draw attention. Their existence is almost forgotten, and that's exactly what lets them survive.

Their main goal is to maintain and perfect their Humanity, never letting the Beast fully swallow the self. But there's a second goal too: a secret war against the Followers of Set. The Setites believe the Children of Osiris are extinct, and the Children do everything they can to keep that belief alive. They don't seek glory, power, or a domain. They're waiting for the moment when the heart grows light enough to take the final step.
Children of Osiris.types: Seekers of Golconda, repentant vampires, monks, mystics, former members of various clans, spiritual hermits, secret enemies of the Setites, temple keepers, ascetics, exiles, fighters against the Beast

discipline_description.Disciplines of the Birth Clan: The Children of Osiris retain access to the Disciplines of their original clan. Their former blood doesn't disappear, but its power is now subordinate to their new spiritual path.
discipline_description.Bardo: The Children of Osiris's spiritual Discipline. It's tied to purification, self-control, the struggle against the Beast, and mystical practices that lead a vampire toward preserving Humanity and seeking Golconda.

Children of Osiris.bane: The bane of their birth clan. The Children of Osiris retain the same clan bane they carried before joining the faction. Their path doesn't undo the curse of the blood — it only offers a chance to learn to live with it without surrendering to the Beast.
Children of Osiris.playstyle: Spiritual discipline, stealth, self-control, and secret war. The Children of Osiris suit characters who struggle against their vampiric nature, seek Golconda, avoid faction politics, and act as hidden enemies of the Setites. Their strength lies not in open power, but in endurance, faith, hidden knowledge, and the ability not to break.
Children of Osiris.conflict: Humanity vs. the Beast. Redemption vs. damnation. Renouncing the clan vs. the impossibility of escaping the blood. Secret war vs. spiritual purification. The wish to greet the dawn vs. the need to first become worthy of that end.
archetypes: Seeker of Golconda — has devoted their immortality to defeating the Beast
Repentant Predator — joined the temple after crimes they can no longer justify
Secret Enemy of Set — hunts the Followers of Set while remaining a myth to them
Temple Keeper — lives in a hidden haven and guards the teaching of Osiris
White Monk — follows strict rites of purification, asceticism, and self-control
Former Camarilla Politician — left the faction game after realizing power doesn't save the soul
Setite Refugee — found protection and new meaning among the Children of Osiris
Student of Bardo — studies the spiritual Discipline as a path to inner freedom

Daughters of Cacophony.description:
The Daughters of Cacophony are a bloodline of song, obsession, and a voice that can change the mind. They're called Sirens, and the nickname fits them with frightening precision: their singing can be beautiful, soothing, maddening, or destructive, but it's never just music.

They became an independent bloodline in the 19th century, although vampires who tried to hold onto their sanity through music and song existed long before that. Learned Kindred argue about their origin: some see in them a Toreador legacy because of their passion for art, others suspect a connection to the Malkavians because of the madness their voice causes, and still others find a resemblance even to the Ventrue. But the name of the first Daughter of Cacophony has remained unknown.

All Sirens share one thing: the song. They hear it always. For some it's an inner music, for some a synesthesia, for some almost a hallucination. It guides them, distracts them, comforts them, torments them, and keeps them from finally falling apart. A Daughter of Cacophony doesn't simply sing — she exists through singing.

Most Sirens are women, but on the outside they can be anyone: a penniless drifter with a guitar, a punk vocalist, a salon diva, an opera prima donna, a club star, or a strange, quiet girl humming under her breath even while blood is being spilled all around her.

The major factions often underestimate the Daughters of Cacophony. There are few of them, they're scattered, they seem detached, and they rarely seek power. But those who've listened to a Siren's songs for long enough know: music changes people. Sometimes imperceptibly. Sometimes irreversibly.
Daughters of Cacophony.types: Singers, opera divas, street musicians, punk vocalists, jazz performers, club stars, mad muses, lone artists, vampires of the stage, wandering Sirens

discipline_description.Presence: Supernatural charm and stage presence. The Daughters of Cacophony use it to hold an audience's attention and inspire admiration, dependency, and emotional openness.
discipline_description.Melpominee: The unique power of song. Lets the Sirens use their voice to affect the mind, emotions, and body of listeners: to inspire, drive mad, break the will, or turn a performance into a weapon.
discipline_description.Fortitude: Supernatural endurance. Helps the Daughters withstand pain, danger, the pressure of the stage, and the consequences of their own inner cacophony.

Daughters of Cacophony.bane: Never-ending inner music. The Daughters of Cacophony constantly hear a song — as a synesthesia, an intrusive melody, or an auditory hallucination. It guides them through immortality, but distracts them from the world around them. The difficulty of all perception checks increases by two, and a Siren's Awareness rating can never be higher than three.
Daughters of Cacophony.playstyle: Social influence, the stage, emotion, and vocal effects. The Daughters of Cacophony excel at performances, manipulating moods, breaking others' concentration, and creating an almost mystical dependency on their art. They sense danger around them less well, but once they're allowed to sing, the situation quickly starts playing by their rules.
Daughters of Cacophony.conflict: Art vs. madness. The voice as a gift vs. the voice as a curse. The stage vs. loneliness. A song that preserves sanity vs. a song that destroys it. The wish to be heard vs. the danger of changing the listener forever.
archetypes: Opera Prima Donna — turns the classical stage into a temple of her own voice
Punk Siren — screams the truth so loudly it cracks listeners' minds
Drifter with a Guitar — wanders from city to city, leaving songs and rumors behind
Club Diva — controls the crowd through rhythm, light, and voice
Mad Muse — inspires artists, lovers, and victims to the point of self-destruction
Star with a Fan Herd — feeds on admirers willing to listen to her forever
Lone Singer — hides in a room with perfect acoustics and sings only for herself
Choir of One Blood — senses other Sirens through strange, simultaneous performances

True Brujah.description:
The True Brujah are a bloodline of cold reason, ancient memory, and a lost legacy. They claim that the modern Brujah aren't true descendants of the Brujah Antediluvian: by their account, Troile, the Antediluvian's own child, committed diablerie against his sire and usurped control of the clan.

Where the ordinary Brujah are passion, rage, and ideals, the True Brujah are their unsettling opposite. According to their lore, Brujah himself wasn't a fiery warrior-philosopher, but an unshakeable sage, an introvert and thinker for whom emotion was an obstacle on the path to understanding the world.

The Sages are few not because they were wiped out like the Salubri or the Cappadocians, but because they rarely grant the Embrace. They choose their offspring carefully, usually from among scholars, historians, philosophers, occultists, and people capable of devoting immortality to knowledge rather than power or passion.

Their legendary wound is Carthage. The True Brujah believe that city, where vampires openly ruled over mortals, fell because of treachery by Troile's descendants. After that, the Sages nearly vanished from history, becoming a rumor, an academic footnote, and a mystery to vampire historians.

Under the cover of modern nights, the True Brujah continue their search for knowledge. For access to ancient sources, they might cooperate with Setites, advise the Sabbat, or hide behind the mask of an ordinary Brujah. They're hard to buy, hard to anger, and almost impossible to understand. They aren't without cruelty — it's just that their cruelty doesn't burn. It's cold, precise, and patient.
True Brujah.types: Scholars, historians, philosophers, occultists, academics, archivists, strategists, cold-blooded advisors, researchers of time, hidden heirs of Carthage

discipline_description.Presence: A power of presence stripped of passionate theatrics. The True Brujah use it as cold authority: to make others listen, show respect, and treat their words as a verdict rather than a request.
discipline_description.Potence: Supernatural physical strength. For the Sages, it isn't an expression of rage but a practical tool of self-defense and precise violence, applied only when truly necessary.
discipline_description.Temporis: A rare power tied to the perception and distortion of time. Lets the True Brujah study the flow of moments, slow it, speed it up, or otherwise interact with time as an object of study and power.

True Brujah.bane: Emotional coldness. At the moment of the Embrace, the True Brujah lose a significant part of their emotional capacity, and their detachment only deepens with the years. The difficulty of all Conscience and Resolve checks increases by two points, up to a maximum of 10. Raising Conscience, Resolve, Humanity, and the Paths of Enlightenment costs twice the usual experience.
True Brujah.playstyle: Knowledge, analysis, cold-blooded influence, and control over time. The True Brujah suit researcher, advisor, archivist, and strategist characters who operate through information, patience, and precise calculation. They're poorly suited to emotional leadership and impulsive rebellion, but excel at the long game and intellectual superiority.
True Brujah.conflict: Reason vs. emotion. Truth vs. estrangement. The Brujah legacy vs. Troile's usurpation. The pursuit of knowledge vs. moral decay. Cold objectivity vs. the need to preserve one's Humanity.
archetypes: Academic Vampire — moves in university circles, hunting for rare knowledge
Historian of Carthage — obsessed with uncovering the truth about the lost city's fall
Hidden Sage — passes as an ordinary Brujah, never revealing their true origin
Sabbat Consultant — helps dangerous allies in exchange for access to forbidden places and archives
Cold Philosopher — studies immortality, the Beast, and time as abstract categories
Source Hunter — travels between havens, libraries, and forgotten domains
Master of Temporis — treats time as a discipline, a weapon, and a subject of research
Heir of the Stolen Clan — believes the modern Brujah are descendants of a usurper and a thief

Cappadocians.description:
The Cappadocians are the lost clan of death, necromancy, and quiet scholarly horror. Their history proves the great law of the vampire world: immortality doesn't mean forever.

The clan's founder, known as Cappadocius, didn't seek to rule his descendants as a tyrant. Instead, he gave them a task: to seek answers to the greatest questions of life, death, and unlife. What awaits the soul after the body dies? Is vampiric existence a continuation of life, or its shadow? Can death be understood by someone who no longer belongs to the living?

The Cappadocians were scholars, mystics, theologians, and necromancers. They settled near monasteries, universities, crypts, dungeons, and places mortals tried to avoid. They cared not for power or luxury, but for the knowledge that could be drawn from every corpse, every grave, every ghost, and every dying king.

In their golden age, the Gravediggers were a full-fledged great clan. They could be found across all of Europe: at the courts of Princes, in monastery libraries, in university circles, among theologians, executioners, soldiers, hermits, and grave robbers. They understood that death comes for everyone, and that's exactly why they considered it the greatest teacher of all.

But openness and curiosity proved their undoing. The Cappadocians took an interest in the Venetian house of Giovanni — a mortal family that knew how to handle the spirits of the dead almost as deftly as they handled money. The Giovanni slowly rose within the clan, until Augustus Giovanni reached the very top, diablerized Cappadocius, and began hunting down his descendants.

By modern nights, only fragments remain of what was once a great clan: the Harbingers of Skulls and other rare remnants of the old blood. The Cappadocians vanished as a clan, but their questions remained. And every necromancer, every ghost, every open grave still whispers their name.
Cappadocians.types: Necromancers, scholars, monks, theologians, mystics, gravediggers, hermits, executioners, soldiers, philosophers of death, advisors to Princes, researchers of the afterlife

discipline_description.Necromancy: The mystical art of death and spirits. The Cappadocians use it to commune with the dead, study the afterlife, probe the soul, and gain power over what remains after life ends.
discipline_description.Fortitude: Supernatural endurance. Lets the Gravediggers endure pain, decay, isolation, dangerous research, and long nights spent among the dead.
discipline_description.Auspex: Heightened perception and mystical insight. Helps them see what's hidden, sense a spiritual presence, read the signs of death, and notice what slips past both the living and the dead.

Cappadocians.bane: A deathly appearance. The Cappadocians look more like living corpses than most vampires. Their skin grows pale and ever more deathlike with age, their hair often darkens after the Embrace, and the elders take on a withered, bony, almost cadaverous look. Young Cappadocians can still pass for mortal with some effort, but for the old Gravediggers it's almost impossible.
Cappadocians.playstyle: Research, necromancy, the mysteries of death, and grim scholarship. The Cappadocians suit mystic, advisor, archivist, spiritual-guide, and afterlife-researcher characters. They excel at knowledge, occult investigation, contact with the dead, and the long intellectual game, but their appearance and obsession with death make ordinary social life difficult.
Cappadocians.conflict: Knowledge vs. demise. Acceptance of death vs. the desire to understand and overcome it. Scholarship vs. naivete. Openness to new allies vs. the risk of betrayal. Immortality vs. the inevitability of an end, even for a clan.
archetypes: Necromancer Monk — studies death through prayer, silence, and conversations with spirits
University Scholastic — seeks answers in books, anatomy, philosophy, and forbidden theology
Prince's Advisor — serves as a spiritual guide and grim analyst at court
Practicing Gravedigger — knows cemeteries, crypts, and bodies better than any library
Ghost Hunter — explores the afterlife through contact with the dead
Purge Survivor — one of the clan's rare fragments, hiding from the Giovanni
Philosopher of Charon — prepares for their own final death as for one last conversation
Tragic Mentor — passes on knowledge, knowing their clan has nearly vanished

Kiasyd.description:
The Kiasyd are a bloodline of alien knowledge, moonlit pallor, and impossible courtesy. Formally, they're considered a branch of the Lasombra: the connection to Oblivion is too obvious to deny. But their appearance, their strange powers of Mytherceria, and their painful vulnerability to iron all hint at a heritage that doesn't fit the ordinary laws of the blood.

The Kiasyd are called Outsiders not just because of how they look. They genuinely seem like beings standing half a step apart from the world of Kindred. Tall, thin, unsettlingly calm, their skin gleaming in moonlight, with pupil-less almond eyes and slightly pointed ears, they look less like vampires than like beings that happened to take on a vampire's shape.

Most Kiasyd prefer solitude, books, and research to politics, wars, and nighttime intrigue. Even as members of the Sabbat, they often serve it in name only, devoting their true loyalty to their own libraries, maps, manuscripts, artifacts, and riddles no one else can even phrase correctly.

Their havens aren't just lairs, but carefully arranged closed worlds: libraries, archives, comfortable rooms, rare vitae, flawless security, and strict rules of hospitality. An invited guest may be received by a Kiasyd with impeccable courtesy. An uninvited one may be destroyed for a single touch of a book, a door, or an object they had no right to touch.

Many rumors swirl around the Kiasyd: that they feed only on the blood of children, the fae, or virgins; that they aren't descendants of the Lasombra at all, but demonic or fae changelings; that their appearance is a sign they aren't truly Kindred at all. The Kiasyd themselves rarely bother to deny any of it. To them, other people's superstitions are just one more curious subject for observation.
Kiasyd.types: Scholars, archivists, librarians, occultists, philosophers, museum curators, antiquity collectors, researchers of the fae, recluses, strange aristocrats, keepers of forbidden books

discipline_description.Dominate: Control of mind and will. The Kiasyd use it with cold precision: to maintain order in their haven, force guests to follow the rules, and shut down any attempt to interfere with their research.
discipline_description.Oblivion: The darkness of the Lasombra, expressed through the Kiasyd's alien nature. Lets them command shadows, hide, defend their domain, and turn the space around them into a moonlit-black trap.
discipline_description.Mytherceria: A strange power of otherworldly perception and impossible truths. The Kiasyd use it to deceive the senses, reveal what's hidden, probe the riddles of reality, and create effects other Kindred tend to chalk up to fae or demonic nature.

Kiasyd.bane: An alien appearance and vulnerability to iron. The Kiasyd are far too conspicuous: their height, slight build, moon-pale skin, pupil-less eyes, and pointed ears make ordinary disguise among mortals almost impossible. They're also painfully sensitive to iron: simply touching an iron object can trigger a frenzy resistance check, and cold iron weapons deal aggravated damage to them.
Kiasyd.playstyle: Research, occultism, shadow, control, and a strange social distance. The Kiasyd suit scholar, archivist, advisor, and secret-keeper characters who operate through knowledge, discipline, hospitality, and closed havens. They excel at intellectual play and mystical investigation, but their appearance and distaste for prolonged contact with mortals complicate ordinary city life.
Kiasyd.conflict: Knowledge vs. estrangement. Hospitality vs. territorial paranoia. Lasombra by blood vs. something else by nature. Solitude vs. service to the Sabbat. Courtesy vs. inhuman coldness. The desire to understand the world vs. the impossibility of fully belonging to it.
archetypes: Moonlit Librarian — keeps a vast collection of books, maps, and manuscripts in a closed haven
Courteous Outsider — impeccably polite to guests, and lethal to anyone who breaks the rules
Occult Researcher — studies the fae, shadows, demons, ancient languages, and impossible phenomena
Sabbat Recluse — formally serves the faction, but keeps their true loyalty for their own research
Artifact Collector — hunts rare objects, not always explaining why they need them
Lasombra Heir — tries to understand where the blood of the Shepherds ends and another legacy begins
Master of the Impossible House — has built a library-haven best entered only by invitation
Reality Mystifier — uses Mytherceria so that others come to doubt their own perception

Blood Brothers.description:
The Blood Brothers aren't a clan in the usual sense, or even full Kindred. They're ritually manufactured Sabbat cannon fodder: quasi-vampires assembled from mortal bodies, blood, Tzimisce sorcery, and renegade Tremere practices.

They aren't Embraced through the normal process. They're created in groups — circles — usually of three to ten beings. The victims are killed, stripped of their former individuality, have their appearance altered until they're nearly indistinguishable from one another, and are then bound into a single fighting organism. After the ritual, almost nothing of the original person remains.

A circle of Blood Brothers functions like a small collective mind. They feel each other, suffer for each other, move as a unit, and barely think outside their connection to the rest. Their frightening power lies not in individuality, but in synchronization. One Frankenstein is dangerous. A circle is already a weapon.

The Sabbat uses them for simple, brutal, fast tasks: assaults, ambushes, guard duty, killings, and sudden crushing blows. The Blood Brothers are loyal, strong, and ask almost no questions. But that's exactly their limitation: without a commander they improvise poorly, don't grasp complex intrigue, and tend to reduce any uncertainty to a single question — watch, or kill.

Their existence is kept secret not out of shame, but out of pragmatism. If enemies learn how the Blood Brothers work, they might find a way to break their bond, get around their blunt loyalty, or turn them against the Sabbat. The Frankensteins are terrifying precisely as long as they appear without warning, strike fast, and leave no surviving witnesses.
Blood Brothers.types: Ritual fighters, Sabbat shock troops, collective soldiers, quasi-vampires, identical twins, bodyguards, cannon fodder, living weapons, fanatically obedient circles

discipline_description.Potence: Raw supernatural strength. The Blood Brothers use it as a melee weapon: smashing doors, bones, barricades, and whatever else their commander orders destroyed.
discipline_description.Sanguinus: The circle's unique power of blood-bond. Lets the Blood Brothers act as a single organism, share sensation, strengthen one another, and turn a group of identical fighters into one synchronized monster.
discipline_description.Fortitude: Supernatural endurance. Makes the Frankensteins durable soldiers, able to withstand wounds, pain, and the fire of battle long enough to carry out an order.

Blood Brothers.bane: No true offspring, and shared pain. The Blood Brothers cannot grant the Embrace: any such attempt ends in the mortal's death. They also feel the wounds of their circle's other members. When one Blood Brother takes a health penalty, the rest of the circle suffers that same effect on the next turn. If several brothers are wounded at the same time, only the strongest of the new penalties is passed on.
Blood Brothers.playstyle: Group combat, assaults, guard duty, and synchronized violence. The Blood Brothers excel as a single fighting unit, especially under the command of a clever vampire. They're poorly suited to solo play, intrigue, social camouflage, and complex decisions. Their strength isn't individuality — it's the circle.
Blood Brothers.conflict: The self vs. the circle. Obedience vs. freedom. The strength of unity vs. total dependence on orders. A human past vs. a ritually erased memory. Brotherhood vs. the factory-made manufacture of living weapons.
archetypes: Identical Trooper — part of a circle built for fast strikes and clean sweeps
Bishop's Bodyguard — stands beside the commander, waiting for the order to kill
Frankenstein of Detroit — a former mortal whose face is now worn by several beings at once
Twin Circle — a group of nearly indistinguishable fighters, told apart only by scars and wounds
Silent Enforcer — asks no questions, only repeats the order within the circle
Broken Individual — a rare ritual flaw who retained fragments of their former self
Guardian Circle — created not for war, but to protect a haven, temple, or Sabbat laboratory
Collective Monster — sees itself not as a separate being, but as a single flesh in many bodies

Lamia.description:
The Lamia are a vanished bloodline of plague, martial loyalty, and a dark maternal revelation. They were not merely allies of the Cappadocians, but their blades, their bodyguards, and the last wall between the Gravediggers and those who wished them final death.

According to legend, the bloodline's founder, known as Lamia, was a daughter of Lilith herself, the Dark Mother. The Cappadocian elder Lazarus saw her performing a sacred rite in Lilith's honor, and, struck by her fury, her beauty, and her devotion, granted her the Embrace. But in the moment of death and rebirth, Lilith sent Lamia a vision of the future — so terrible that even mighty Lazarus fled after hearing only a few words of it.

From then on, the Lamia became protectors of the Cappadocians. Almost every sufficiently influential Gravedigger had at least one Gorgon in their retinue. They didn't inherit their patrons' deathly appearance, but they carried something far more terrible: the Seed of Lilith, a deadly disease spread through their bite.

Because of this, the Lamia fed with caution and discipline. Their hunger could become an outbreak of plague, and their blood a weapon, a curse, and a sacred legacy all at once. Over time, the Gorgons developed their own path of Necromancy, tied to the philosophy of the four humors, fusing the body, death, disease, and mystical knowledge.

When the Giovanni betrayed the Cappadocians and began wiping out the clan, the Lamia fulfilled their purpose to the very end. They died defending their patrons. Lamia herself fell at the hand of Augustus Giovanni shortly after Cappadocius's death, and the last known Gorgon was destroyed in 1718 during a Blood Hunt. Their line vanished, but the memory of them remained — women who stood between death and death, and did not step aside.
Lamia.types: Bodyguards, warrior women, necromancers, servants of Lilith, Gorgons, protectors of the Cappadocians, plague-bearers, graveyard guardians, occult killers, grim mentors

discipline_description.Potence: Supernatural strength. The Lamia use it as a bodyguard's weapon: to break bones, keep enemies away from their patrons, and end a fight before a threat can reach the Cappadocian.
discipline_description.Necromancy: The mystical art of death. For the Lamia, it's tied not only to spirits but to the body, disease, the humors, and a sacred understanding of mortality through the legacy of the Dark Mother.
discipline_description.Fortitude: Supernatural endurance. Lets the Gorgons withstand wounds, poison, pain, disease, long vigils, and brutal fights in which they often served as the last line of defense.

Lamia.bane: The Seed of Lilith. A Lamia's bite carries a deadly disease. Any vessel a Gorgon feeds from must make a Stamina check — the difficulty is higher for men and lower for women. On a failure, the victim is infected with an illness resembling the Black Death and dies within a few days. A vampire who drinks a Lamia's blood becomes a carrier of the disease themselves for as long as that vitae remains in their body.
Lamia.playstyle: Protection, close combat, necromancy, and grim discipline. The Lamia suit bodyguard, warrior, and occult-guardian characters who stand beside more scholarly or politically important allies. They excel at combat, endurance, and intimidation, but their cursed blood makes feeding dangerous and demands constant self-control.
Lamia.conflict: Loyalty vs. one's own fate. Protecting the Cappadocians vs. service to Lilith. Hunger vs. the risk of spreading plague. A lineage of women warriors vs. the world of clan politics. A revealed future vs. the impossibility of changing a tragic end.
archetypes: Gorgon Bodyguard — stands between her patron and any threat
Daughter of Lilith — holds the cult of the Dark Mother above clan obligations
Humor Necromancer — studies death through blood, disease, the body, and mystical medicine
Graveyard Guardian — prefers the company of the dead to that of the living or the undead
Cappadocian Warrior — serves the Gravediggers as sword, shield, and warning
Plague-Bearer — fears her own bite no less than her enemies fear her blood
Survivor of the Coup — a rare Gorgon who went into hiding after the Giovanni uprising
The Last Lamia — a relic of a vanished line, carrying the memory of a clan she couldn't save

Lhiannan.description:
The Lhiannan are a vanished bloodline of forest fury, pagan magic, and a vampiric bond with the spirits of nature. They claimed that their founder, known as the Old Witch, struck a pact with a forest spirit trying to escape human greed, fire, and the axe.

That spirit watched people destroy forests, burn trees, and tear down its home. Merging with an immortal predator became its way of surviving. That's how the Lhiannan came to be — vampires carrying a fragment of an ancient forest being in their blood.

Each new Druid received a part of that mystical power, but the price was high: by siring a descendant, a sire lost a fragment of the spirit and grew weaker. That's why the Lhiannan almost never sired offspring. They created children only out of loneliness, necessity, or when a mortal made a truly powerful impression on them.

The Lhiannan lived far from cities, in thickets, huts, natural shelters, and remote settlements. They could be healers, witches, seers, forest monsters, or guardian spirits that local mortals both feared and venerated. Their bond with nature ran deeper than the Gangrel's: the forest wasn't hunting ground to them, but part of their own soul.

But this dual nature made them vulnerable. The Lhiannan were tied to their native ground, traveled poorly, and almost always lived alone. With the spread of Christianity, the rise of the Inquisition, and conflicts with Kindred who embraced the new order, the Druids became too scattered to survive.

By the end of the 14th century, the bloodline was considered destroyed. But in modern nights, the Gangrel increasingly tell stories of beings in the forests of Europe: powerful vampires covered in bloody symbols, speaking to trees, and vanishing into the thicket. Perhaps the Lhiannan survived the Dark Medieval period. Or perhaps the forest spirit simply found a new vessel.
Lhiannan.types: Druids, forest witches, healers, seers, hermits, pagan shamans, grove keepers, forest predators, territorial spirits, cursed defenders of nature, blood mystics

discipline_description.Animalism: A bond with animals, the Beast, and the wild. The Lhiannan use it not just for power over beasts, but as the forest's natural language, through which they speak with the living world around them.
discipline_description.Presence: Supernatural presence. In the Druids, it manifests as ancient forest dread and pagan awe: mortals near them feel they're standing before something old, hungry, and sacred.
discipline_description.Ogham: The Lhiannan's enigmatic Discipline, tied to bloody symbols, nature spirits, and the power of native soil. It lets the Druids live far from cities, draw strength from the forest, and turn their territory into a mystical weapon.

Lhiannan.bane: An inhuman nature, and an attachment to their native land. The Lhiannan are, in part, nature spirits, and that's hard to hide: the difficulty of Auspex checks meant to uncover their true nature is reduced by two, and ordinary mortals near them feel uneasy. In addition, if a Lhiannan stays away from their native land for too long, they begin to weaken: every week, the dice pool for all their checks is reduced by one d10, down to a minimum equal to their Stamina. After returning home, this effect fades within a few hours.
Lhiannan.playstyle: Territorial magic, wilderness survival, pagan dread, and control of forest space. The Lhiannan are strong on their native ground, among trees, beasts, spirits, and superstitious mortals. They fit poorly into city politics, long journeys, and faction games, but in their own forest they can be nearly impossible to catch.
Lhiannan.conflict: Nature vs. civilization. Spirit vs. vampiric blood. Native land vs. the need to leave. Solitude vs. the need for an heir. Pagan memory vs. the Christian world. Protecting the forest vs. the Druid's own predatory nature.
archetypes: The Old Witch — lives at the edge of the forest and knows the names of spirits best left undisturbed
Blood Druid — covers their body in Ogham symbols and works magic through blood and wood
Forest Protector — defends a village or grove, demanding blood and reverence in return
Backwoods Healer — heals, curses, and guides mortals while remaining half a myth to them
Lone Predator — can't stand other Lhiannan nearby and fiercely guards their territory
Child of the Forest Spirit — feels like not a vampire, but a continuation of an ancient natural will
Inquisition Survivor — hid for centuries in remote thickets while the world forgot their name
The New Covenant — a vampire chosen by the forest spirit after the old bloodline vanished

Nagaraja.description:
The Nagaraja are a bloodline of necromancy, cannibalism, and secret wars beyond the world of the living. They're called the Flesh Eaters, and even among Kindred the name is spoken with disgust: blood isn't enough for a Nagaraja. To survive, they need flesh.

Their origin is buried in rumor. Some believe they arose in the Middle East from a death cult whose members ritually ate human flesh and knew how to open gates into the world of the dead. Others link them to the Giovanni, the Tremere, or the last fragments of the Cappadocians. The Nagaraja themselves are in no hurry to explain anything. The less others know about their past, the easier it is to remain a useful, frightening story.

Most Nagaraja are educated, withdrawn, and dangerously practical. Among them are former physicians, priests, antiquarians, exorcists, occultists, and ghost hunters. They're interested in death not as a philosophical abstraction, but as territory, as a resource, and as a battlefield.

Some Flesh Eaters are tied to Tal'mahe'Ra — the true Black Hand, a mysterious faction operating in the Land of the Dead. There, in a city built beyond the Shroud, the Nagaraja are more common than in the world of the living. Those who remain on this side are usually independent: taking jobs, gathering secrets, solving ghost problems, and carefully covering the traces of their feeding.

The Nagaraja are nearly extinct. There are few of them, they have too many enemies, and every hunt inevitably leaves a corpse behind. But that's exactly what makes them so dangerous. The Nagaraja can't afford to be careless. Every one of them is a hungry specialist in death, who knows the price of a body, a soul, and silence.
Nagaraja.types: Necromancers, cannibals, occultists, priests of death, physicians, antiquarians, exorcists, ghost hunters, agents of Tal'mahe'Ra, hidden independents, corpse eaters

discipline_description.Dominate: Control of mind and will. The Nagaraja use it to manage servants, silence witnesses, command mortal helpers, and resolve problems before they become public.
discipline_description.Necromancy: The mystical art of death and spirits. For the Nagaraja, it's not just knowledge of the dead but a practical tool for dealing with ghosts, the Land of the Dead, corpses, and secrets that must never return to the living.
discipline_description.Auspex: Supernatural perception. Lets them see what's hidden, notice spiritual traces, understand the nature of death, and recognize threats from both the living and the Restless Dead.

Nagaraja.bane: A hunger for flesh. The Nagaraja must feed not only on blood, but on the flesh of their victims. For every night without fresh flesh, the character's physical dice pools are reduced by one d10. Each point of flesh consumed reduces this penalty by one d10 and replenishes one point of blood. An adult human body contains ten points of flesh. Every point of flesh a Nagaraja consumes inflicts one unavoidable point of aggravated damage on the victim. The flesh must be relatively fresh, but doesn't need to be alive; some Nagaraja keep preserved corpses in their havens.
Nagaraja.playstyle: Necromancy, stealth, occult investigation, and damage control. The Nagaraja suit characters who deal with ghosts, corpses, secret cults, and dirty problems other Kindred would rather look away from. They excel at knowledge, contact with the dead, and intimidation, but their method of feeding makes every hunt risky, and almost always fatal for the victim.
Nagaraja.conflict: Knowledge of death vs. monstrous hunger. Practicality vs. others' revulsion. The world of the living vs. the Land of the Dead. Independence vs. secret obligations to Tal'mahe'Ra. The need to feed on flesh vs. the need to remain unnoticed.
archetypes: The Flesh Eater — accepts their hunger as the price of existence
Underworld Necromancer — works with spirits, the Shroud, and the secrets of the dead
Cannibal Physician — knows anatomy too well, and uses it while hunting
Agent of Tal'mahe'Ra — serves a mysterious war in the Land of the Dead
Exorcist of the Abandoned House — solves ghost problems for a price best not asked about
Keeper of the Corpse Larder — keeps their haven stocked so fresh flesh is always at hand
Occult Antiquarian — trades in artifacts, relics, and knowledge that smells of the grave
Last of the Hungry — one of the few surviving Nagaraja, too careful to die easily

Noiad.description:
The Noiad are a northern Gangrel bloodline tied to the polar night, the Sami peoples, and the lights of the aurora borealis. Where almost no other Kindred ever reached, amid the frozen tundra, the long nights, and endless snow, the Wanderers found a way to exist where a vampire has almost nothing to feed on.

To the Noiad, the northern lights weren't a natural phenomenon but a sacred text. They watched its shifting colors, read the will of ancient gods in them, and passed that will on to their mortal followers. The nomadic Sami tribes lived before them in fear and awe, seeing the Noiad as god-like protectors, shamans, and mentors.

In return, the Noiad protected their tribes from outside threats — people, monsters, spirits, predators, and other Kindred. They guarded the encampments, spoke with animals, interpreted dreams, watched for omens, and returned to the tribe after the long polar day, during which mortals kept watch over their sleeping bodies.

But their patronage wasn't pure blessing. Like any vampires, the Noiad brought stagnation with them. They didn't allow the Sami to change, turned away outsiders, killed or banished anyone who might disrupt the old order, and in the end led their mortal herds ever further north — away from the best lands, the reindeer, and a new age.

It's unclear whether they were guided by genuine revelations from the northern lights, or by fear of Christianity, the Lasombra, and inevitable change. But their choice nearly destroyed the very people they swore to protect. Here lies the Noiad's tragedy: they were shepherds, shamans, and protectors — yet remained predators who mistook their own authority for the will of the gods.
Noiad.types: Northern shamans, Sami patrons, tundra wanderers, dream interpreters, seers, stargazers, protectors of encampments, night shepherds, spirits of the polar night, Gangrel hermits

discipline_description.Animalism: A bond with animals, especially reindeer, wolves, draft animals, and messengers of the tundra. The Noiad use it to protect their tribes, communicate with animals, and pass messages through tamed or ghouled beasts.
discipline_description.Protean: The Gangrel's bestial and earthen nature. For the Noiad, it's a way to survive the extreme north: hiding in frozen ground, taking on a predator's traits, and becoming part of the tundra.
discipline_description.Auspex: Supernatural perception and prophetic interpretation of signs. The Noiad use it to read the northern lights, dreams, the stars, tracks in the snow, and hidden threats approaching their tribes.

Noiad.bane: Dependence on human blood. According to their lore, the Noiad became so bound to their role as patrons of the Sami tribes that they lost the ability to feed on animal blood. They can drink the blood of humans and vampires, but animal blood no longer satisfies their hunger. This dependence ties them especially closely to the mortal herds they both protect and exploit.
Noiad.playstyle: Survival in the far north, shamanism, protecting the herd, and prophetic perception. The Noiad are strong in the wilderness, among nomads, animals, and harsh conditions where other Kindred quickly become helpless. They fit poorly into city politics and accept change badly, but in their own tundra they can be almost god-like figures.
Noiad.conflict: Protector vs. predator. Patronage vs. control. Sacred duty vs. fear of change. The tribe as family vs. the tribe as herd. The ancient omens of the northern lights vs. a new age that cannot be stopped.
archetypes: Interpreter of the Lights — reads the will of the gods in the fires of the northern sky
Tribal Shaman — leads the tribe through dreams, omens, and night councils
Shepherd of the Sami — protects mortals, because without them he himself cannot survive
Guardian of the Polar Day — entrusts his sleeping body to the tribe during the long sun
Messenger of Beasts — communicates with other Noiad through animal messengers
Cold Prophet — sees future threats, but mistakes revelation for fear
Outsider-Banisher — kills anyone who brings change to the tundra
Child of the Midnight Sun — considers himself part of a family other Kindred could never understand

Harbingers of Skulls.description:
The Harbingers of Skulls are a small bloodline of death, vengeance, and ancient necromancy. They appeared as if out of the mists of the past, already old, powerful, and frighteningly composed, as though the last five centuries hadn't been spent asleep in torpor, but listening intently to the lessons of death itself.

The Lazarenes themselves claim that, all this time, they were dead. Looking at them, it's easy to believe: their skin is shriveled and drawn tight over bone, their teeth are frozen in a death's grin, and their faces resemble skulls bleached by time. They look less like vampires who resemble corpses, and more like corpses who, for some reason, decided to come back.

Their origin remains a mystery. Many suspect a connection to the Samedi, or to the destroyed Cappadocians, but the Harbingers don't discuss the past with anyone who hasn't earned an answer. They arrived in modern nights already carrying a purpose, a memory, and a hatred old enough to need no explanation.

The Sabbat quickly recognized their value. Where the Sword of Caine had no Giovanni, the Harbingers gave it power over ghosts. They banish vengeful spirits, turn the dead into spies and bodiless slaves, and solve problems that can't be stabbed, burned, or torn apart with claws. But the Lazarenes don't see this as service. They see it as a favor, one that will someday have to be repaid.

The Harbingers don't dissolve into Sabbat packs or play at brotherhood. They keep to themselves, communicate through enslaved ghosts, travel by unfathomable means, and accumulate debts. Their vengeance against the Giovanni isn't a flash of rage, but a cold process stretched across centuries. When the day of reckoning comes, money, blood, and the influence of moneylenders won't save them from those who have already once returned from death.
Harbingers of Skulls.types: Ancient necromancers, avengers, Lazarenes, withered elders, Giovanni hunters, lords of ghosts, corpse hermits, shroud-prophets, fragments of the Cappadocians, spies of the dead

discipline_description.Necromancy: Power over death, ghosts, and the afterlife. The Harbingers use it to banish spirits, enslave the dead, extract secrets from beyond the Shroud, and turn death itself into an instrument of vengeance.
discipline_description.Fortitude: Supernatural endurance. The Lazarenes look like withered corpses, but their ancient blood lets them withstand pain, destruction, and long centuries of waiting.
discipline_description.Auspex: Heightened perception and mystical insight. Helps the Harbingers see what's hidden, sense the presence of the dead, read the traces of death, and know in advance when a debt will turn into a reckoning.

Harbingers of Skulls.bane: A corpse-like appearance. A Harbinger's skin always stays deathly pale, no matter how much blood they drink. Every Lazarene looks like a withered corpse: their Looks rating is always zero, and any check requiring that trait automatically fails.
Harbingers of Skulls.playstyle: Necromancy, ancient secrets, ghosts, vengeance, and hidden influence. The Harbingers suit elder, grim-advisor, spirit-lord, and cold-avenger characters. They excel at working with the dead, secret communication, occult knowledge, and the long game, but their appearance nearly rules out a normal existence among mortals.
Harbingers of Skulls.conflict: Vengeance vs. a purpose for existing. Death vs. return. Duty to the Sabbat vs. the Lazarenes' own plans. The Cappadocian past vs. the modern power of the Giovanni. A terrifying appearance vs. the need to act covertly.
archetypes: Lazarene of the Shroud — returned from death and no longer considers themselves part of the living world
Cappadocian Avenger — accumulates debts and prepares a reckoning for the Giovanni
Lord of Ghosts — uses the dead as spies, servants, and weapons
Elder from the Past — knows more than they say, and remembers wars others consider legend
Sabbat Necromancer — helps the Sword of Caine only because one day they'll demand payment
Corpse Hermit — lives in a crypt, slaughterhouse, or abandoned lab, surrounded by blood and fresh bodies
Traveler of the Dead Roads — moves by means other Kindred can't comprehend
Debt Collector — treats every favor as a future noose around someone else's neck

Salubri.description:
The Salubri are a nearly extinct bloodline of healing, prophecy, and a heavy spiritual burden. They were once a full clan, descended from Saulot — a warrior, judge, and sage who grew weary of the Kindred's endless violence and traveled east in search of a different path.

Saulot returned changed: calm, thoughtful, humble — and with a third eye on his brow. His descendants split into two branches: warriors and healers. Some protected, others healed, but for centuries both served as advisors, bodyguards, prophets, and spiritual guides to Kindred and mortals alike.

Then Saulot was destroyed. The Tremere found his body in torpor and committed diablerie upon him, claiming the power of one of the ancient clans for themselves. The Salubri never recovered. They were hunted, slandered, and turned into a frightening tale — of diablerists who devour souls, of traitors, demon-worshippers, and secret Sabbat allies.

The cruelest part of this slander is that it uses a grain of truth. Many Salubri do commit diablerie on their own sires after the Embrace — not out of a hunger for power, but as part of a strange and somber transfer of blood, in which the elder willingly steps aside for their descendant. To the outside world, this looks like proof of monstrousness. To the Salubri themselves, it looks like a tragic duty.

Under the cover of modern nights, if the rumors are true, only seven of them remain. They seek no power, build no factions, and fight for no domains. They try to live long enough to reach Golconda, pass their blood on to a worthy successor, and disappear. The Salubri know: sometimes being a monster doesn't mean drinking by force, seeking revenge, or ruling — it means carrying what you've seen with three eyes.
Salubri.types: Healers, prophets, seekers of Golconda, spiritual guides, empaths, former teachers, repentant sinners, bodyguards, exiles, wandering sages, victims of slander

discipline_description.Obeah: The Salubri's unique power, tied to healing, the easing of suffering, spiritual insight, and the third eye. It makes the Cyclops not merely healers, but conduits between pain, repentance, and the possibility of inner purification.
discipline_description.Fortitude: Supernatural endurance. The Salubri use it to endure wounds, persecution, long years of wandering, and the weight of a path on which they have almost no one to trust.
discipline_description.Auspex: Heightened perception and mystical insight. Lets them see hidden pain, lies, spiritual states, and dangers other Kindred would rather not notice.

Salubri.bane: An inability to feed by force with ease. The Salubri have a hard time satisfying their hunger from those who don't give blood willingly. If a Cyclops feeds from a victim against their will, they lose one point of Willpower. This makes their hunting harder, and underscores the bloodline's central inner conflict: they remain vampires, but cannot act like predators without consequence.
Salubri.playstyle: Healing, empathy, spiritual seeking, secrecy, and survival under slander. The Salubri suit characters who struggle for their Humanity, seek Golconda, help others, and are forced to constantly hide from those who believe Tremere propaganda. They excel at support, insight, and moral drama, but are vulnerable due to their scarcity, their reputation, and the difficulty of hunting.
Salubri.conflict: Mercy vs. vampiric hunger. Golconda vs. the curse of the blood. Truth vs. Tremere slander. Healing vs. diablerie as inheritance. The third eye as insight vs. the third eye as a mark for hunters.
archetypes: Exiled Healer — helps those who are afraid even to say their clan name aloud
Seeker of Golconda — builds their entire immortality around spiritual liberation
Sire's Heir — committed willing diablerie and now carries their elder's memory
Three-Eyed Prophet — sees pain, fate, and lies more deeply than they'd like to
Repentant Villain — was Embraced in the hope of finding a path to redemption
Hidden Cyclops — passes as a member of another clan to survive one more night
Enemy of the Tremere — doesn't seek open revenge, but will never forgive Saulot's murder
Last of the Seven — one of the few surviving Salubri, forced to choose a successor with painful care

Samedi.description:
The Samedi are a bloodline of decay, contract killing, and crude Caribbean horror. If the Nosferatu inspire revulsion, and the Harbingers of Skulls evoke withered death, the Samedi look as though death didn't just come for them, but left them to rot in the heat, and then changed its mind.

Their origin is tied to the Caribbean, to voodoo, and to the figure of Baron Samedi — the loa of death, of graveyards, of obscene laughter, and of ancestral souls. Vampires of this bloodline bear his name, and often resemble him not just in their corpse-like appearance, but in temperament: crude, biting, irreverent, and dangerously intemperate.

The oldest known member of the bloodline is a vampire who calls himself Baron Samedi. He still takes part in the affairs of the Stiffs, but reveals almost nothing about the past — especially about his strange feud with Augustus Giovanni. Only one thing is clear: at some point there was a deal, or a conflict, between the Samedi and the Giovanni, and its aftermath still smells of the grave.

The Samedi are often mistaken for kin of the Nosferatu because of their monstrous appearance, but their Thanatosis points to a deeper connection to death, to dead bodies, and possibly to necromantic lines like the Giovanni or the Cappadocians. The Stiffs themselves don't see any need to clear up the question. The less others know, the more dearly the answers can be sold.

In Kindred society, the Samedi most often work as thugs, bodyguards, and hired killers. Unlike the Assamites, they don't dress up killing as religion or duty. It's a job. They take contracts against Nosferatu and Giovanni reluctantly, and only for enormous pay — not out of mercy, but because they respect, and fear, those who know the dirty secrets of the underground and of death all too well.
Samedi.types: Hired killers, thugs, Stiffs, graveyard predators, Caribbean mystics, bodyguards, corpse specialists, voodoo monsters, loners, grim jokers, underground mercenaries

discipline_description.Obfuscate: The power of shadow, stealth, and disappearance. The Samedi use it to creep up on victims, slip away after a kill, and exist beyond the notice of anyone not ready to look at a walking corpse.
discipline_description.Fortitude: Supernatural endurance. Lets the Stiffs endure wounds, pain, the fire of battle, and the physical breakdown of a body that already looks like a mistake of nature.
discipline_description.Thanatosis: A terrible power of decay and dominion over dead flesh. The Samedi use it to control corpses, accelerate decomposition, maim enemies, and turn death into a filthy, physical tool.

Samedi.bane: A rotting appearance. The Samedi look like decomposing walking corpses: their skin peels, their flesh oozes, gaping holes mark where a nose and ears should be, their eyes are sunken, and their teeth show through a rotten grin. Their Looks rating is always zero, and any check using that trait automatically fails.
Samedi.playstyle: Stealth, killing, intimidation, physical endurance, and dominion over decay. The Samedi suit loner, mercenary, and grim dirty-work-specialist characters. They excel at eliminating targets, night ambushes, contact with the world of death, and psychological pressure, but their appearance almost entirely rules out a normal Masquerade among mortals.
Samedi.conflict: The body as corpse vs. the self as living intent. Money vs. honor. Independence vs. the need for contracts. Laughing at death vs. an eternal existence steeped in decay. Fear of the Giovanni and Nosferatu vs. the need to work alongside them.
archetypes: Stiff for Hire — takes payment for killing and doesn't pretend it's some sacred duty
Servant of the Baron — keeps ties to the bloodline's Caribbean roots and its oldest member
Graveyard Killer — sets up havens among morgues, slaughterhouses, cemeteries, and dead bodies
Rotten Joker — answers others' revulsion with crudeness, laughter, and threats
Thanatosis Master — studies decay as a weapon, a craft, and an art form
Underground Loner — risks conflict with the Nosferatu by choosing havens beneath the city
Debtor of an Old Deal — knows there's a secret between Baron Samedi and the Giovanni best left undug
Mummy Without Wrappings — a dry, withered Samedi, more like an ancient corpse than rotting meat

Blood Leech.description: The character feeds on the blood of other Kindred: hunting them, forcing them to give up vitae, or collecting it as payment. This is morally acceptable to him, but is considered a dangerous and often forbidden practice in vampire society.

Consensualist.description: The character never drinks blood without the victim's consent. He might pose as an employee of a donation center, a member of the "real vampire" subculture, or even tell the truth and risk the Masquerade. This approach is more common among former sex workers, activists, and lawyers.

Osiris.description: The character is an object of worship: a celebrity or cult leader. He feeds on the blood of fans or followers who give it willingly. He always has access to vessels, but this lifestyle draws the attention of the authorities, religious organizations, and the Camarilla.
notes: Split three dots between the "Fame" and "Herd" background traits.
Split two dots between Folkloric Block flaws and the "Enemy" flaw.

Siren.description: The character drinks blood during sex, or under the guise of it, using seduction, Disciplines, and human lust. He might be a star of sex clubs or a master manipulator, but doubts and a distorted sense of intimacy often lurk beneath the surface. Former partners can become either a support system or a source of trouble.

disadvantages.name: Enemy (Weak)
disadvantages.details: a rejected lover or jealous partner

Sandman.description: The character breaks into people's homes and feeds on the sleeping, relying on Stealth or Disciplines. If the victim doesn't wake up, they'll never know. Reclusive, cautious vampires who want to avoid unwanted attention and social risk prefer this approach.

Alleycat.description: The character is an aggressive hunter who takes blood by force. He attacks victims, intimidates them, or uses Disciplines without any concern for consent. This style is often tied to a criminal past or a life of violence.

Cleaver.description: The character secretly feeds on the blood of his own family or loved ones, maintaining a bond with them while trying to lead something resembling a normal life. This requires constant lying and control, and carries a serious risk of breaching the Masquerade.

Bagger.description: The character doesn't hunt people directly, but obtains donated blood through theft, purchase, or the black market. This approach requires caution, connections, and access to resources, but it lets him avoid direct violence.

Scene Queen.description: The character feeds on members of a particular subculture in which he's fully integrated. They accept him as one of their own, and some even adore him. He maintains a carefully crafted image that lets him stay part of the scene and hunt without raising suspicion.

Farmer.description: The character feeds exclusively on animal blood, refusing to harm people. This choice requires constant discipline and is often accompanied by an inner hunger, but it reflects a desire to hold on to humanity.
restriction: Not available at Blood Potency 3 or higher

Thaumaturgy.description: Thaumaturgy is the blood sorcery of the Kindred: a flexible and dangerous Discipline created by the Tremere from an alchemical union of human magic and vampiric vitae. It includes many paths and rituals, letting an adept control blood, fire, the elements, objects, the weather, plants, technology, the mind, and the very structure of the curse.

system.type: non-linear discipline / school of blood sorcery
system.theme: vitae, rituals, blood, the elements, fire, will, magical paths, secret knowledge
system.structure: Thaumaturgy is divided into paths and rituals. The first path chosen becomes the primary path and grows together with the character's Thaumaturgy level. Secondary paths are purchased separately with experience.
system.starting_rule: Upon gaining their first point of Thaumaturgy, the character chooses one path, gains that path's level-1 power, and one level-1 ritual.
system.path_limit: No path's rating can exceed 5. The Thaumaturgy rating itself can exceed 5.
system.secondary_paths: A secondary path can be chosen once the primary path reaches level 2. The primary path must always be higher than any secondary path. A secondary path can only reach level 5 after the primary path has already reached 5.

activation.success: On a success, the power takes effect.
activation.failure: On a failure, the power doesn't take effect.
activation.botch: A botch can lead to catastrophic consequences: loss of Willpower, a reduced Willpower rating, spontaneous combustion, loss of control over the summoned power, or some other dangerous effect.

system.rituals: Rituals aren't purchased with experience, but obtained through the story: through mentors, grimoires, deals, research, and the clan hierarchy. The maximum level of a ritual that can be learned cannot exceed the character's current Thaumaturgy level.

Path of Blood.description: The most common path among the Tremere, devoted to fundamental power over vitae. It lets a thaumaturge analyze blood, force it to be spent, temporarily boost their own Generation, steal blood, and boil it in a victim's veins.

Taste of Blood.description: The thaumaturge touches the target's blood and learns information about their vitae.
Taste of Blood.effect: Reveals how much blood remains in the victim's body. If the victim is a vampire, the thaumaturge can learn how long ago they fed, their Generation, and whether they've committed diablerie.
Taste of Blood.success_scaling: The more successes, the more precise and detailed the information.

Blood Rage.description: With a touch, the thaumaturge forces another Kindred to spend blood.
Blood Rage.effect: Each success forces the victim to spend 1 Blood point however the sorcerer wishes: for example, to boost a Physical attribute or activate a Discipline.
Blood Rage.special: Blood spent this way isn't limited by the victim's Generational cap.
Blood Rage.frenzy_risk: Each Blood point spent this way increases the difficulty of frenzy resistance checks by 1.
Blood Rage.limits: The thaumaturge cannot use this power on themselves.

Blood of Potency.description: The thaumaturge concentrates their own vitae and temporarily lowers their Generation.
Blood of Potency.effect: One success lowers Generation by 1 step for 1 hour. Additional successes can be split between extending the duration and lowering Generation further.
Blood of Potency.limits: Can be used no more than once per night.
Blood of Potency.diablerie: If the character becomes a victim of diablerie, the effect ends immediately, and the diablerist gains the benefits of the victim's true Generation.
Blood of Potency.embrace: A childe's Generation is determined by the thaumaturge's true Generation, not their temporarily boosted one.
Blood of Potency.aftereffect: When the power ends, the maximum blood supply returns to normal; any excess blood is expelled and lost.

Theft of Vitae.description: The thaumaturge steals the victim's blood at a distance: it erupts from their body and is mystically absorbed into the sorcerer's own.
Theft of Vitae.effect: Each success steals 1 Blood point from the victim.
Theft of Vitae.limits: Vitae stolen this way cannot form a blood bond.
Theft of Vitae.masquerade: Using this in public looks supernatural and counts as a Masquerade breach.

Cauldron of Blood.description: With a single touch, the thaumaturge boils the victim's blood right in their veins.
Cauldron of Blood.effect: Each success boils 1 Blood point and deals 1 wound that risks final death.
Cauldron of Blood.resistance: The victim may use Fortitude on the soak check.
Cauldron of Blood.mortal_effect: A single success is enough to kill a mortal. A ghoul with Fortitude may survive if they soak the damage.

Elemental Mastery.description: A path of limited control over non-living and inanimate objects. It lets a thaumaturge draw strength from matter, speak with the spirits of objects, animate objects, transform into an object, and summon elementals.

Elemental Strength.description: The thaumaturge draws strength from the earth and surrounding objects.
Elemental Strength.effect: On a success, the character gains 3 temporary dots to split among their Physical attributes.
Elemental Strength.extension: Spending 1 Willpower point extends the effect by 1 more turn.
Elemental Strength.limits: The power can only be used again after the current effect ends.

Wooden Tongues.description: The thaumaturge speaks with the spirits of objects and gains their memories.
Wooden Tongues.effect: Lets the character learn what an object has 'perceived.' The more successes, the more detailed and precise the information.
Wooden Tongues.note: The spirits of objects perceive the world differently: they may remember fire, weight, movement, or touch, but won't necessarily notice what matters to a vampire.

Animate the Object.description: The thaumaturge animates objects and controls them through subconscious force of will.
Animate the Object.effect: An object no larger than an adult human can be animated. The maximum number of animated objects equals the sorcerer's Intelligence.
Animate the Object.limits: The object must be within sight. The object's actions are limited by its shape.
Animate the Object.extra_cost: If activation scores 3 successes or fewer, an additional Willpower point must be spent.

Object Form.duration: until the end of the night
Object Form.description: The vampire takes the form of an object roughly their own size and mass.
Object Form.effect: The character could become, for example, a table, a statue, or a bicycle, but not a house or a pen.
Object Form.success_requirement: At least 3 successes are needed to use Disciplines and perceive what's happening nearby.

Summon Elemental.description: The thaumaturge summons a spirit of an element: fire, air, earth, or water.
Summon Elemental.requirement: A sufficient amount of the corresponding element must be nearby.
Summon Elemental.elemental_stats: All of the elemental's Physical and Mental attributes equal 3. Each additional success on activation grants the elemental 1 additional attribute point, chosen by the Storyteller.
Summon Elemental.control_roll: After summoning, the elemental must be subdued with a Manipulation + Occult check at a difficulty of 4 + the number of successes on activation.

control_table.botch: The elemental immediately attacks the thaumaturge.
control_table.failure: The elemental is set free and attacks the first person it meets, or leaves.
control_table.1 success: The elemental doesn't attack the thaumaturge.
control_table.2 successes: The elemental is good-natured and will grant one request for a worthy price.
control_table.3 successes: The elemental will grant one reasonable request for free.
control_table.4 successes: The elemental will carry out any request that doesn't threaten its existence.
control_table.5 successes: The elemental will carry out any request, even one that's lethally dangerous to itself.

Hands of Destruction.description: A destructive path, more often associated with Sabbat thaumaturges. It governs aging, rot, acid, atrophy, and the accelerated decay of matter.

Aging.description: With a touch, the thaumaturge accelerates the aging of an inanimate object, a corpse, or dead organic matter.
Aging.effect: The object ages roughly 10 years per minute, as long as the thaumaturge maintains physical contact.

Wood Warping.description: With a glance, the thaumaturge bends, warps, dries out, or swells wooden objects.
Wood Warping.effect: Can affect wooden objects within sight.
Wood Warping.mass: The total mass of the objects can't exceed 25 kg for each Blood point spent on activation.
Wood Warping.special: More than 1 Blood point can be spent, but not more than the per-turn blood-spending limit.

Corrosive Touch.cost: 1 Blood point per dose of acid
Corrosive Touch.description: The thaumaturge secretes a caustic liquid through their skin, harmless to themselves.
Corrosive Touch.effect: One dose of acid burns through about 5 mm of steel, or 5–10 cm of wood.
Corrosive Touch.combat: In combat, the thaumaturge's unarmed attacks deal aggravated damage until the end of the turn.
Corrosive Touch.immunity: The sorcerer is immune to their own acid.

Atrophy.description: The thaumaturge instantly withers a victim's limb, turning it into an almost mummified appendage.
Atrophy.resistance: The victim resists with a Stamina + Athletics check at difficulty 8, and must score more than two successes.
Atrophy.effect: On a failed resistance check, the chosen limb is crippled. One success on the resistance check gives +2 to the difficulty of checks made with that limb; two successes give +1.
Atrophy.vampire_healing: A vampire may spend 5 Blood points to restore the limb.
Atrophy.mortal_effect: For mortals, the effect is irreversible.
Atrophy.limits: Only affects limbs and parts of them, not the head or torso.

Wither to Dust.description: With a touch, the thaumaturge accelerates the aging of a living or non-living organism.
Wither to Dust.effect: Each success ages the victim's body by 10 years.
Wither to Dust.resistance: The victim may attempt a Stamina + Courage check at difficulty 8, and must score more successes than the thaumaturge.
Wither to Dust.vampire_effect: Affects a vampire more weakly: causes pallor, gauntness, and a temporary 1-point reduction to Looks until the next night.

Lure of Flames.description: A path for summoning mystical fire. As long as the fire stays in the thaumaturge's hands, it doesn't burn them or cause Rötschreck; once released, it becomes ordinary fire and goes out of control.

system.placement: The number of successes determines how precisely the fire appears. 1 success — fire in the hand. 5 successes — precise appearance at any point in sight. Each success can be treated as roughly +10 meters to the radius of precise summoning.
system.damage_note: A soak check against the fire is only possible with Fortitude.
system.release: As long as the fire isn't released, it deals no damage. Once released, it acts as ordinary fire.

Candle Flame.description: A small tongue of flame, candle-sized.

Inferno.description: Hellish flame at this path's maximum strength.

Movement of the Mind.description: A path for telekinetically moving objects and beings by the power of thought and blood. At higher levels it allows levitation, flight, and throwing objects as weapons.

system.duration: Each success lets the chosen object be moved for 1 turn. After that, the activation check can be repeated without spending blood. 5+ successes let the object be moved until the end of the scene.
system.living_target: If the target is a living being, an opposed Willpower check against the victim's Willpower is made each turn.
system.levitation: From path level 3, the thaumaturge can levitate and move themselves at running speed.
system.throwing: From path level 4, objects can be thrown at a target with force equal to the Movement of the Mind level.

Half a Kilogram.description: Lets the character move very light objects.

10 Kilograms.description: Lets the character move small objects.

100 Kilograms.description: Lets the character lift large objects and levitate themselves.

250 Kilograms.description: Lets the character move heavy objects and throw them as weapons.

500 Kilograms.description: Lets the character move very heavy objects.

Neptune's Might.description: A path for controlling water. It lets a thaumaturge read the past of bodies of water, create watery shackles, turn liquids into water, raise flowing walls, and dehydrate the living and non-living alike.
Neptune's Might.specialization: From level 3, a specialization can be chosen: fresh water or salt water. The difficulty of powers using the chosen type of water is reduced by 1, and increased by 1 for the other type. Blood counts as neither fresh nor salt water.

Sea's Eye.extra_roll: Awareness + Occult (for fine details, at the Storyteller's discretion)
Sea's Eye.description: The thaumaturge looks into still water and sees what happened in the body of water, on its surface, or on the shore, from the water's point of view.
Sea's Eye.limits: Only works with standing water: lakes, ponds, puddles. Doesn't work with the ocean, a river, sewage, or a glass of wine.
Sea's Eye.details: For fine details, the Storyteller may require an Awareness + Occult check.

Water Prison.description: The thaumaturge animates water and commands it to imprison the victim.
Water Prison.requirement: Enough water is needed; 10–15 liters can already form shackles.
Water Prison.escape: The victim breaks free with a Strength + Potence check at difficulty 8, if they score more successes than the thaumaturge did on activation.
Water Prison.limits: A single victim can only be in one Water Prison at a time, but the thaumaturge can create several prisons.
Water Prison.scarcity: If water is scarce, the activation difficulty increases by 1.

Dilute the Blood.description: The thaumaturge turns liquids into water, most often the victim's blood.
Dilute the Blood.activation: Requires touching the victim, the liquid, or its container.
Dilute the Blood.effect: Each success turns 1 Blood point of the victim's blood into water.
Dilute the Blood.mortal_effect: A single success is enough to kill a mortal within a few minutes.
Dilute the Blood.vampire_effect: The vampire loses blood and takes a Health penalty as though they'd taken the same amount of damage. The water leaves the body at a rate of 1 point per hour, but the blood doesn't return.
Dilute the Blood.other_liquids: With the Storyteller's permission, other liquids can be transmuted too; the difficulty is usually 1 lower if the liquid isn't dangerous or mystical.

Wall of Water.description: The thaumaturge creates a straight wall of water that holds back the living, the dead, and even incorporeal beings.
Wall of Water.activation: Requires touching the surface of a standing body of water.
Wall of Water.size: Each success can be traded for 3 meters of the wall's length or height.
Wall of Water.crossing: To pass through the wall, a supernatural being must make a Willpower check at difficulty 9 and score at least 3 successes.
