options.full_description: You're familiar with the ideas of Golconda and can discuss them.
options.mechanic: Once per story, you may ask the Storyteller whether a specific action would bring you closer to Golconda.

options.full_description: You possess a text describing an alternative understanding of Golconda as the path of the perfect predator.
options.mechanic: Once per story, you gain +3d10 to a social check related to discussing or arguing about Golconda.

options.full_description: You follow Saulot's teaching and strive for enlightenment through control over the Beast.
options.mechanic:
Each time you voluntarily enter frenzy, make a note.
On your next bout of frenzy, you automatically succeed at the resistance check.

options.full_description: You're learning to control your need for blood.
options.mechanic:
Once per game session, you may lower Hunger by 1 without feeding (but not below 1).
This doesn't replace feeding and grants no resonance.

options.full_description: You've reached a state that lets you temporarily withstand the vampire's curse.
options.mechanic:
Once per story, you may stand in sunlight without taking damage.

Afterward, the following night you automatically enter a hunger frenzy.

BAHARI.name: Bahari (Path of Lilith)
BAHARI.description: The Bahari, or Lilim, are Kindred who follow the Path of Lilith. They venerate Lilith as the progenitor of vampires and the source of all vitae. In their view, Lilith was the first vampire, and Caine merely a usurper. The Bahari seek enlightenment through pain, struggle, and overcoming their own weaknesses. They reject traditional moral norms, exalt carnal pleasure, and see suffering as a path to power and understanding. To other Kindred, the Bahari can be tormentors, mentors, lovers, or fanatics.

options.full_description: The Bahari inspire fear and discomfort in other Kindred, especially followers of the Church of Caine.
options.mechanic: Once per story, you may add +2d10 to an Intimidation check if the target is a devout Cainite who knows of your beliefs.

options.full_description: Pain is a path to enlightenment. Through physical suffering, the Bahari achieve deep self-understanding and self-control.
options.mechanic: Once per game session, you may inflict 1 aggravated wound on yourself to remove 1 point of Stress (superficial or aggravated).

options.full_description: The Bahari believe the greatest sacrifice is destroying one's own offspring. It's an act of absolute strength and devotion to the Path.
options.mechanic: If you commit diablerie on your own childe, you gain +3d10 to the Humanity + Blood Potency check made to absorb their Disciplines.

options.full_description: The Bahari link power to the act of birth and to the body itself. Blood drawn from a female womb is considered especially potent.
options.mechanic: Once per story, after drinking such blood, you gain +2 to Stamina or Resolve until dawn (raising Health or Willpower by 2 accordingly).

options.full_description: You're deeply bound to Lilith's curse. Caine's curse weakens in you, while another, older one emerges.
options.mechanic:
You can stay awake for 1 hour after dawn and 1 hour before dusk.
You don't need to Rouse the Blood to engage in sexual activity.
However, you make a frightening impression (as with the "Predatory Mien" flaw).
The difficulty of checks made against attempts to slander or banish you is reduced by 1.
Any vampire who uses Auspex on you gets a severe migraine: their Resolve and Willpower are reduced by half your Resolve (rounded up) for one scene.

HAVEN_ADVANTAGES.name: Haven Advantages
HAVEN_ADVANTAGES.description: Additional haven upgrades that enhance its functions.

options.mechanic: +1d10 to knowledge checks (one specialty)
Escape difficulty 5 (or higher)
+1d10 to Science or Alchemy checks
+1d10 to resist supernatural influence
+2d10 to Medicine checks
4 guards + 1 commander
+1d10 to detecting intrusions
+1d10 to escape and stealth
+2d10 to social checks with guests
hidden weapons available
+2d10 in fitting situations

ALLIES.name: Allies
ALLIES.description: Allies are mortals who help you of their own free will. They can be friends, family, or organizations. They aren't under your control and have their own interests, but are willing to support you from time to time.

options.full_description: You have a weak ally who is rarely useful in dangerous situations.
options.mechanic:
Effectiveness: weak mortal.
Reliability: shows up to help about half the time.

options.full_description: You have an ordinary ally or a small group.
options.mechanic:
Effectiveness: ordinary mortal, or a group of weak ones.
Reliability: arrives in 1–10 hours.

options.full_description: You have a strong ally or an organized group.
options.mechanic:
Effectiveness: gifted mortal, or a group of ordinary ones.
Reliability: arrives as fast as possible.

options.full_description: You have a very strong ally or a powerful organization.
options.mechanic:
Effectiveness: a desperate mortal, or someone supernaturally gifted.
Reliability: arrives as fast as possible.

RETAINERS.name: Retainers
RETAINERS.description: You have one or more helpers who carry out your errands. These can be ghouls, dependents, or loyal mortals. They require motivation and may betray you if mistreated.

options.full_description: You have a simple helper with no special skills.
options.mechanic: Uses the traits of a weak mortal. Carries out simple tasks.

options.full_description: You have a reliable servant — a ghoul, a subordinate, or someone close to you.
options.mechanic: Uses the traits of an ordinary mortal or ghoul. Can carry out more complex tasks.

options.full_description: Your retainer can act independently and handle tasks without your oversight.
options.mechanic: Uses the traits of a gifted mortal or an empowered ghoul. Can handle tasks autonomously.

SUBSTANCE_USE.name: Substance Use
SUBSTANCE_USE.description: Some vampires know how to use substances in the blood to their advantage. You need to choose a substance in advance, and a category of traits it affects (Physical, Social, or Mental).

options.full_description: You gain a boost when you feed on blood containing the substance you need.
options.mechanic: If your last victim's blood contained the needed substance, you gain +1d10 to checks in the chosen trait category.

TRIUMVIRATE.name: Triumvirate
TRIUMVIRATE.description: You're connected to the ideas of the Triumvirate — the philosophical alliance of Michael, the Dracon, and Antonius, who ruled Constantinople in its Golden Age. Their utopia sought to unite vampires of differing views into a harmonious society. Though their alliance fell apart, some Kindred still believe the Triumvirate can be reborn. You either seek to revive that dream, or watch closely those who try to.

options.full_description: You know the true significance of Constantinople and its historical role.
options.mechanic: Once per story, you may ask the Storyteller a question about Constantinople's past and receive a truthful answer.

options.full_description: You've studied the political and social models Antonius created.
options.mechanic:
+2d10 to Politics checks when running a domain.

Once per story, you can stop a conflict at court.

options.full_description: You follow Michael's philosophy and strive for enlightenment.
options.mechanic:
+1d10 to Insight checks when assessing another's Beast.

Once per story, you may spend 1 Willpower so that another vampire can reroll up to 3 dice when resisting frenzy.

options.full_description: You consider yourself a student of the Dracon, and may know how to find him.
options.mechanic:
The Dracon is your Mawla (5 dots).
He helps you with spiritual matters and mastering Disciplines.

options.full_description: You're part of a prophecy about the rebirth of the Triumvirate and a new vampire utopia.
options.mechanic:
Once per story, you can remove up to 5 Stains before a remorse check.

You must act in accordance with this prophecy.

FIRST_INQUISITION.name: First Inquisition
FIRST_INQUISITION.description: You've deeply studied the history of the First Inquisition — the period when Kindred were destroyed en masse by religious fanatics. You know the causes behind it, the hunting methods used, and the mistakes that led to the fall of many domains. This knowledge lets you avoid similar threats, and even turn modern hunter forces against your enemies, though it's always a risk.

options.full_description: You know the key events and tragedies tied to the First Inquisition.
options.mechanic: Once per story, you can get information from the Storyteller about a matter related to the medieval Inquisition and its activities.

options.full_description: You know the names of vampires who tried to use the Inquisition for their own ends, and can use this knowledge.
options.mechanic: Once per story, you can learn from the Storyteller the name of such a Kindred's descendant located in your domain, and use it as leverage.

options.full_description: You have contacts within a fanatical group that considers itself the true Inquisition.
options.mechanic:
You have access to a contact (level 2).
Once per story, you can turn to them for help or information.

options.option_name: A Fact of the Second Inquisition
options.full_description: You have a source of information inside the modern Second Inquisition or church structures.
options.mechanic:
You have an informant inside the organization.
You don't control them, but you can get information or try to feed them disinformation.

options.full_description: You know a place even the Second Inquisition fears.
options.mechanic:
Once per story, you can use this place to completely escape the Inquisition's pursuit.

However, this place is tied to an unknown threat, determined by the Storyteller.

BLOODSTREAM.name: Bloodstream
BLOODSTREAM.description: "Bloodstream" is an international network for trading vessels and researching blood. The organization transports people with special resonances, holds them, and studies them. It doesn't care about the fate of mortals — only the value of their blood. You might be a client, a member, or someone with access to its resources.

options.full_description: You have access to Bloodstream's services and can order suitable vessels.
options.mechanic: Once per story, you can order a vessel with the blood properties (resonance) you need.

options.full_description: You possess Bloodstream's records of its blood experiments.
options.mechanic:
+1d10 to checks:
— Investigation
— Alchemy
— Medicine
— Science
(when studying blood)

The time to craft Thin-blood Alchemy formulas (levels 2–3) is halved.

options.full_description: You know the location of a Bloodstream farm where vessels are held.
options.mechanic:
The farm counts as a Herd (3 dots).
You can use it to feed once a week (unless you've taken it over completely).
You may attempt to steal a vessel or make use of its services.

options.full_description: You have access to Bloodstream's secured transport — an armed convoy van.
options.mechanic:
You can safely transport Kindred or cargo.
The transport is armored and armed.
Lets you avoid most road-based threats.

options.full_description: You have a deep understanding of blood's properties and know how to analyze and use them.
options.mechanic:
+2d10 to checks made to identify a resonance.

In addition: split 3 dots between:
— Contacts
— Allies
— Haven

Once per story, you can learn the properties of the most valuable vessel currently available.

THIN_BLOODED.name: Thin-blooded Merits
THIN_BLOODED.description: The thin-blooded have unique traits. Each merit grants 1 dot and must be balanced by a flaw.

options.full_description: You know thin-blood alchemy.
options.mechanic: You gain 1 level of Alchemy and 1 formula.

options.full_description: The sun doesn't damage you, but it weakens you.
options.mechanic: During the day your Health is halved, and you lose your vampiric abilities.

options.full_description: You're resistant to physical damage.
options.mechanic: Slashing and piercing damage counts as superficial.

options.full_description: You have a patron.
options.mechanic: Counts as a Mawla (1), but you're obligated to serve.

options.full_description: You look like a living human.
options.mechanic: You have a pulse, can eat, and can pass cursory checks.

options.full_description: You can sire offspring.
options.mechanic: You can create thin-blooded vampires and form bonds.

options.full_description: You have an innate Discipline.
options.mechanic: You gain 1 level of a chosen Discipline.

options.full_description: You have the Anarchs' support.
options.mechanic: Counts as a Mawla (1), as long as you remain useful to them.

RUDI.name: Rudi
RUDI.description: Rudi is a young but rapidly rising Anarch, a defender of the oppressed among both mortals and Kindred. He fights for equality, tears down outdated structures, and inspires others to resist. You're connected to his movement — as an ally, a follower, or an active participant.

options.full_description: Like Rudi, you're inspired by the Gangrel's liberation from the Camarilla, and feel a surge of strength.
options.mechanic: Once per story, you may reroll one check of any skill, if the action is directed against the Camarilla or its authority (physically or ideologically).

options.full_description: You genuinely believe in protecting the weak and are willing to risk yourself for others.
options.mechanic:
When an ally from your coterie is in danger:
— you may act without penalties from fear or doubt
— the Storyteller may grant you an advantage in the scene (such as +1d10 or an easier check)

(the exact bonus is determined by the Storyteller based on the situation)

options.full_description: You actively defend clan Gangrel's interests and act as their voice.
options.mechanic:
+1d10 to all social checks when dealing with the Gangrel.

You can arrange meetings between the Gangrel and the Camarilla:
— Charisma + Politics check
— difficulty set by the Storyteller

You may also be held responsible for the Gangrel's actions in others' eyes.

options.full_description: You're connected to a radical group of Rudi's followers — aggressive revolutionaries.
options.mechanic:
The Bear Pack counts as a Mawla (3 dots).

Once per story:
— you gain 1 automatic success
— when trying to inspire Anarchs or mortals to riot

The Camarilla fears you:
— this may grant bonuses or cause problems depending on the situation.

options.full_description: You command part of a diverse and unstable revolutionary force.
options.mechanic:
You gain 6 dots to split between:
— Allies
— Influence
— Contacts

You can direct a crowd:
— protests
— riots
— pressure on authority

But:
— the consequences are unpredictable
— the Storyteller may complicate the situation or introduce chaos

LOOKS.name: Looks
LOOKS.description: Not every vampire looks like an actor from a popular TV series. Some are far more beautiful, and others noticeably less so. These modifiers only apply when the character can be seen. The Storyteller decides exactly how they apply in a social conflict.

options.full_description: You're attractive and make a pleasant impression on people.
options.mechanic: Pools for all social checks that Looks could affect increase by 1d10.

options.full_description: Your appearance is exceptional and makes a powerful impression.
options.mechanic: Pools for all social checks that Looks could affect increase by 2d10.

LINGUIST.name: Linguist
LINGUIST.description: Every character knows their native language and the chronicle's language. Linguist lets you know additional languages.

options.full_description: Each dot of this merit adds one additional language the character can speak, read, and write. From a game standpoint, language change over time is usually ignored (for example, Old and Modern English are considered the same language). The Storyteller may raise the difficulty of checks involving ancient texts or unusual dialects.
options.mechanic: For each dot, you gain +1 language (full fluency: speaking, reading, writing).

DESCENDANT_OF_ZELIOS.name: Descendant of Zelios
DESCENDANT_OF_ZELIOS.description: You carry on the legacy of Zelios — a legendary architect and geomancer among the Nosferatu. His knowledge of space, defense, and sacred geometry still influences the world of Kindred. You know how to build, how to destroy, and how to read the hidden structure of the world — from buildings to mystical ley lines.

options.full_description: Your haven is a feat of engineering, combining security with thoughtful architecture.
options.mechanic:
You gain 2 dots to split between:
— Escape Routes for your haven
— an Advanced Security System

In addition:
— your haven has hidden passages and defensive systems

Drawback:
— someone knows the weak points or layout of your haven (Storyteller's discretion)

options.full_description: You understand the structure of buildings and know how to bring them down without complex tools.
options.mechanic:
Given simple tools (a crowbar, a sledgehammer, etc.) and time:
— you can collapse a building

Approximate time:
— a private house: ~4 days
— a bank: ~6 days
— a skyscraper: ~9 days

Exact timing is set by the Storyteller

options.full_description: You're known among Kindred as a specialist in havens and fortifications.
options.mechanic:
Once per story:
— you receive one moderate debt from another vampire

In addition:
— you know the location of some influential Kindred's havens

This can grant:
— an advantage
— or create danger (if the information gets out)

options.full_description: You've built a complex network of underground tunnels and passages in your domain.
options.mechanic:
You have access to a labyrinth beneath the domain:
— sewers
— tunnels
— basements

Effect:
— if you reach an entrance during a chase, your pursuers lose your trail

Restriction:
— the labyrinth is too complex → you cannot use it as a haven
— there's a risk of getting lost (Storyteller's discretion)

options.full_description: You can sense the world's hidden lines of energy — ley lines.
options.mechanic:
If your haven sits at the intersection of ley lines:
— upon waking, roll 2 dice for your Blood check
— take the better result

In addition:
— ley lines may grant other effects (Storyteller's discretion)

Risk:
— other Nosferatu may punish you for revealing this information

HIGHER_CLANS.name: Higher Clans
HIGHER_CLANS.description: You belong to one of the so-called Higher Clans — the elite of Kindred who have historically considered themselves above the rest. While this division is formally outdated, many vampires still hold to this hierarchy. You were raised believing you have the right to command others, and that your clan bane is not a curse, but a blessing.

options.full_description: You display your superiority through behavior, manners, and confidence.
options.mechanic:
Once per game session, when trying to earn respect from a "lesser" vampire:
— reroll 1 die

(usually applied to social checks)

options.full_description: You know how to subdue vampires from the Lower Clans, and are used to exploiting that advantage.
options.mechanic:
You have a group of vampires equivalent to a Mawla (2 dots).

When dealing with them:
— +3d10 to Intimidation and Leadership checks

Risk:
— on a severe failure, they may rise up against you
— regaining control will require a full scene

options.full_description: You hold the right to elevate members of the Lower Clans.
options.mechanic:
Once per chronicle:
— you may officially raise the status of a vampire from a Lower Clan

In addition:
— as long as you hold this promise over them, you gain +1d10 to social checks when dealing with that vampire

(the effect works as a lever of influence)

options.full_description: You're seen as a natural-born leader among the Higher Clans.
options.mechanic:
+1d10 to Leadership checks when dealing with Higher Clan vampires who know your lineage.

Once per story:
— members of the Higher Clans back you
— letting you obtain power or a position

Restriction:
— doesn't apply in cases of personal enmity

options.full_description: You consider your clan bane not a weakness, but a manifestation of a higher nature.
options.mechanic:
Once per game session:
— spend 1 Willpower point
— ignore the effect of your clan bane for one scene

DESCENDANT_OF_XAVIAR.name: Descendant of Xaviar
DESCENDANT_OF_XAVIAR.description:
Xaviar was a former Gangrel Justicar, the first to warn Kindred of the threat posed by the Antediluvians. No one listened, and he later met final death under mysterious circumstances. His descendants carry the shadow of that story: guilt, rage, and a drive to learn the truth.

The Gangrel feel they betrayed Xaviar, and now treat his descendants with particular attention. Some walk the path of anarchy, others remain in the Camarilla, but all are connected to this legacy one way or another.

⚠️ Restriction: available only to members of clan Gangrel.

options.full_description: The Gangrel feel a respect toward you that should have belonged to Xaviar.
options.mechanic: You can always find shelter among the domain's Gangrel (if any are present). You gain Status ● among the Gangrel.

options.full_description: You feel a connection to the earth and can sense vampires hidden within it.
options.mechanic: Check: Resolve + Awareness. Lets you determine whether a vampire is buried underground nearby. Difficulty depends on the area searched.

options.full_description: You stayed with the Camarilla and were rewarded for your loyalty.
options.mechanic: Split 4 dots between: Status, Herd, Domain. Anarch Gangrel despise you; the Camarilla supports you.

options.full_description: You've inherited Xaviar's rare bestial form.
options.mechanic:
Once per story: transform into an anthropomorphic bat.
Bonuses:
— +1 to all Physical attributes
— flight
— your bite deals aggravated damage (+1 modifier)

options.full_description: You survived contact with an Antediluvian, and were changed by it.
options.mechanic:
Once per story:
— locate any Gangrel
— draw out their vitae
— reduce Hunger to 2

Condition: you must be standing on bare earth (not concrete).

Side effect: paranoia, claustrophobia, a sense of connection to the earth.

DESCENDANT_OF_HELENA.name: Descendant of Helena
DESCENDANT_OF_HELENA.description: You're descended from Helena, a legendary figure of clan Toreador, a symbol of beauty, talent, and allure. Conflicting rumors surround her, but her influence on the clan is undeniable. Her descendants are seen as the embodiment of the Toreador ideal, and many strive to get close to them, even indirectly.

options.full_description: Even mentioning Helena near you strengthens your standing.
options.mechanic: Once per story, by mentioning Helena in conversation with a knowledgeable Kindred, you temporarily raise your Status by 1 point.

options.full_description: You possess real talent, worthy of Helena's legacy.
options.mechanic:
Choose one skill: Craft, Etiquette, or Performance.
The experience cost to raise that skill is halved (round down).

options.full_description: You know how to play the role of the perfect Toreador, creating an atmosphere of luxury and admiration around you.
options.mechanic:
Once per story, you can host an event.
For the duration of the event, your Status or influence among the guests increases by 2 points.

options.full_description: Your appearance and manner create an impression of absolute innocence, regardless of your actions.
options.mechanic: +2d10 to checks aimed at avoiding responsibility or excusing your actions.

options.full_description: You've earned the right to open your own club tied to Helena's legendary establishment.
options.mechanic:
While the club is operating:
— the domain's Chasse rating increases by 2

In addition: split 4 dots between:
— Resources
— Fame
— Status among Kindred

You become a well-known figure in the city.

DESCENDANT_OF_TYLER.name: Descendant of Tyler
DESCENDANT_OF_TYLER.description: You're connected to Tyler's legacy — a legendary revolutionary among Kindred, a symbol of resistance against the tyranny of elders. Her ideas shaped the Anarch Movement, and even now her descendants carry the fight forward. You either continue her work, or strive to surpass her. Others see you as a potential leader of rebellion, but with that comes responsibility for the consequences of revolution.

options.full_description: Your speech can set a crowd ablaze and push it toward violence.
options.roll: Charisma or Manipulation + Leadership or Persuasion
options.mechanic:
Once per story, when inciting mortals to violence:
— add +2d10 to the relevant social check (e.g., Charisma/Manipulation + Leadership/Persuasion)

options.full_description: Others see you as the leader of an uprising, even if you never planned to be.
options.mechanic:
During an uprising:
— your Status among the rioters increases by +2 points
— NPC rioters tend to follow you

The effect lasts as long as the uprising, or preparations for it, continue.

options.full_description: You understand the limits of violence and can stop yourself, even in the Beast's grip.
options.mechanic:
Once per story, while in frenzy:
— you may voluntarily trigger the Brujah clan compulsion
— the frenzy immediately ends
— you suffer no aftereffects (fatigue, disorientation, etc.)

options.full_description: You belong to a secret group of revolutionaries whose goal is to destroy tyranny among Kindred.
options.mechanic:
Once per chronicle:
— you gain the support of the Wrathful's network
— they provide:
   • weapons
   • a haven
   • allies

Effect:
— Allies up to level 5 (for one scene)

Restriction:
— can only be used against a significant target (a Prince, Baron, or equivalent status)
— abusing this makes you their target

options.full_description: You've already helped overthrow a ruler, and continue spreading revolution further.
options.mechanic:
As long as you're actively engaged in revolutionary activity:
— Anarchs automatically follow you
— Anarch Brujah are willing to carry out even dangerous orders

No checks are required if:
— you speak with confidence
— your arguments are logical

Restriction:
— the effect only works as long as you don't start living like the elite (losing your revolutionary image)

STATUS.name: Status
STATUS.description: This is your reputation and standing within a specific Kindred faction. It affects how you're treated, and can grant bonuses in social interactions.

options.full_description: You're known among Kindred and have been introduced to important figures.
options.mechanic: You're recognized and given basic respect.

options.full_description: You have a stable reputation and hold your place in the hierarchy.
options.mechanic: You're taken seriously, and your opinion is considered.

options.full_description: You hold authority and influence part of the faction.
options.mechanic: You can influence the decisions and actions of other Kindred.

options.full_description: You hold an important post in the faction.
options.mechanic: You hold an official title (such as Sheriff or equivalent) and power.

options.full_description: You're among the faction's highest elite.
options.mechanic: You wield maximum influence (such as Primogen or an equivalent figure).

HERD.name: Herd
HERD.description: You have a group of mortals whose blood is regularly available to you. You can lower Hunger without a check, but the herd requires attention and can fall apart if overexploited.

options.full_description: You have a small group of vessels.
options.mechanic:
1–3 vessels.
Once a week, you can lower Hunger by 1 without a check.
You can change one vessel's resonance once a month (Manipulation + Insight check, difficulty 4).

options.full_description: You have a stable group of vessels.
options.mechanic:
4–7 vessels.
Half have a chosen resonance.
Lower Hunger by 2 per week without a check.

options.full_description: You have a well-controlled group.
options.mechanic:
7–15 vessels.
2 types of resonance (selectable each week).
Lower Hunger by 3.

options.full_description: You manage a large group of people.
options.mechanic:
16–30 vessels.
2 types of resonance.
Lower Hunger by 4.
Requires regular oversight and involvement in the story.

options.full_description: You have a vast network of vessels.
options.mechanic:
31–60 vessels.
3 types of resonance.
Lower Hunger by 5.
Greatly increases the risk of a Masquerade breach.

INFLUENCE.name: Influence
INFLUENCE.description: This is your power in a specific sphere of society (for example: the police, the media, organized crime, or a city district). The higher the level, the more people are willing to listen to you, help you, or obey you. Influence works mainly within the chosen sphere and weakens outside it.

options.full_description: You're known and can get attention.
options.mechanic: You're given a hearing and a chance to speak.

options.full_description: You carry a certain weight in society.
options.mechanic: People are willing to do you small favors.

options.full_description: Your name inspires respect and fear.
options.mechanic: Influential people and groups try not to clash with you.

options.full_description: You can give orders directly.
options.mechanic: Subordinates and dependents carry out your instructions.

options.full_description: Your power is so great that people try to please you in advance.
options.mechanic: Others try to anticipate your wishes and act without being ordered.

WEEK_OF_NIGHTMARES.name: Week of Nightmares
WEEK_OF_NIGHTMARES.description: You're connected to the events known as the Week of Nightmares — a catastrophe in which an ancient vampire awoke and nearly destroyed clan Ravnos. The world was swept by omens of Gehenna: the Scarlet Star Antgelios, waves of thin-bloods, and devastation. You either witnessed these events, or studied them so deeply that they became part of your identity. Now you're alert to any sign of the end times.

options.full_description: You've heard and retold the story of the Week of Nightmares so many times that your account has become a polished performance.
options.mechanic:
When telling the story of the Week of Nightmares, you gain +3d10 to Performance checks.
You may be invited to perform at Elysiums.
You draw interest from Kindred, including influential ones.

options.full_description: You stay in contact with surviving members of the Ravnos.
options.mechanic:
You have a group of contacts equivalent to a Mawla (2 dots).
They share information and warn you of dangers.

Once per chronicle, you may try to convince them to create a complex illusion.

If your ties become known, it will draw condemnation.

options.full_description: You personally lived through the Week of Nightmares.
options.mechanic:
You have a reputation as a veteran of those events.

Once per story, you can use this to get a favor from:
— occultists
— historians
— Gehenna believers
— Ravnos

This counts as a moderate debt.

options.full_description: You're able to see Antgelios, the herald of Gehenna, even when others cannot.
options.mechanic:
Spend 10 minutes observing Antgelios.

Once per story, choose an effect:
— reduce Hunger to 2
— gain +1d10 to checks with one Discipline until the end of the night

options.full_description: You possess a vial of blood from the Ravnos Antediluvian.
options.mechanic:
You possess a unique artifact.

What happens when the blood is used is entirely up to the Storyteller.

The effect may be:
— extremely powerful
— dangerous
— irreversible

BLOOD_BOND.name: Blood Bond
BLOOD_BOND.description: Some vampires resist blood bonds better, or break free of them faster. These merits can be combined with flaws.

options.full_description: Your blood resists outside influence.
options.mechanic: For each dot, you gain +1d10 to checks to resist a blood bond.
options.note: Can be taken up to 3 times.

options.full_description: Blood bonds fade from you faster than from other vampires.
options.mechanic: The bond weakens twice a month (at the full moon and the new moon).

options.full_description: You're completely immune to blood bonds.
options.mechanic: A blood bond cannot be formed on you.

DESCENDANT_OF_VASANTASENA.name: Descendant of Vasantasena
DESCENDANT_OF_VASANTASENA.description: You've inherited Vasantasena's philosophy — rejecting blood bonds, refusing coercion, and fighting for Kindred freedom. Her followers turned away from both the Camarilla and the Sabbat, choosing their own path. You possess charisma, madness, and a drive to break the bonds that enslave others.

options.full_description: You feel at home in chaos and instability.
options.mechanic:
Once per game session, in a chaotic situation (a chase, a fight, panic, a disaster):
— reroll 1 die
— without spending Willpower

options.full_description: Your voice affects others on a deep level, bypassing rational thought.
options.mechanic:
Once per story, if the target stops and listens to you in a tense situation:
— they may reroll one check, once
— the next time they find themselves in a similar situation

(the effect works like a suggestion/anchor)

options.full_description: You can sense the existence of a blood bond between vampires.
options.mechanic:
Once per story:
— Resolve + Awareness check (difficulty 4)
— on a success, you detect the presence of a blood bond
— and can sense the other party to the bond

options.full_description: You're able to break a blood bond — an extremely rare and dangerous skill.
options.mechanic:
Once per story:
— drink the blood of the bound vampire (the vassal)
— then enter frenzy
— the blood bond is broken

(the exact consequences and risks are determined by the Storyteller)

options.full_description: You can dismantle someone else's ideology and literally change a vampire's worldview.
options.difficulty: an extended series; successes needed = 2 × the target's Willpower
options.mechanic:
Once per story:
— isolate the target
— carry out a lengthy process of persuasion

Check mechanic:
— a series of checks: Intelligence or Charisma + Insight
— 1 check every 3 nights
— goal: accumulate successes = 2 × the target's Willpower

On success:
— the target's beliefs change (subject to the Storyteller's agreement)

MASK.name: Mask
MASK.description: A Mask is a fully developed cover identity that lets you safely exist among people. The higher the level, the harder it is to expose.

options.full_description: You have a well-crafted cover identity with documents and a history.
options.mechanic: Your Mask passes a background check at the state or regional level.

options.full_description: Your identity is carefully built and withstands serious scrutiny.
options.mechanic: Your Mask passes checks at the national level (police, intelligence agencies).

options.full_description: Your real identity has been completely deleted from every database.
options.mechanic: Officially, you don't exist.

options.full_description: You know how to create cover identities.
options.mechanic: Creating a Mask takes 3 days per dot (alone) or 1 day with help. May draw attention.

RESOURCES.name: Resources
RESOURCES.description: This is your economic status, capital, and access to resources. It determines the character's standard of living, property, and opportunities. Be sure to describe your source of income — it determines how stable your wealth is.

options.full_description: You live paycheck to paycheck, but have basic stability.
options.mechanic: Access to an apartment, a car, and ordinary gear.

options.full_description: You have a stable income and a comfortable standard of living.
options.mechanic: Access to good housing, several cars, and professional gear.

options.full_description: You live at a high standard and have significant resources.
options.mechanic: Access to expensive housing, luxury goods, and resources for a group of people.

options.full_description: You hold serious capital and rare resources.
options.mechanic: Access to a mansion, private transportation, and specialized equipment.

options.full_description: You're practically unlimited in money.
options.mechanic: You can afford almost any purchase or resource.

options.full_description: You cannot read or write, and cannot work with written information.
options.mechanic: Academics and Science skills cannot exceed 1. You cannot take specialties related to modern knowledge.

RIVAL.name: Rival
RIVAL.description: This is a Kindred (or group) who is hostile toward you. It's usually connected to your sire, your bloodline, or your past.

options.full_description: You're opposed by a less influential enemy.
options.mechanic: They get in the way of your affairs and cause trouble.

options.full_description: Your enemy holds significant influence.
options.mechanic: They actively intervene in the story and can cause serious harm.

options.full_description: You're opposed by an extremely powerful enemy.
options.mechanic: This could be a Prince or a major group. They pose a constant threat.

RETAINERS.name: Retainers
RETAINERS.description: You have servants or helpers who work for you. They require attention, motivation, and are not soulless tools.

options.full_description: You attract people who can't let you go. A former retainer keeps following you, trying to come back.
options.mechanic: A former or new clingy retainer periodically appears in the game. They can cause problems, get in the way, or complicate the situation.

HAVEN_DISADVANTAGES.name: Haven Disadvantages
HAVEN_DISADVANTAGES.description: Additional haven problems that make it less safe or comfortable.

options.full_description: Your haven looks suspicious and frightening.
options.mechanic: −2d10 to social checks with guests.

options.full_description: Your haven's location is known to your enemies.
options.mechanic: +2d10 to checks made to track or break into it against you.

options.full_description: There's an uncontrolled supernatural threat in your haven.
options.mechanic:
−1d10 to all checks affected by the issue.
Examples: ghosts, a poltergeist, a curse, a vermin infestation.

HAVEN.name: Haven
HAVEN.description: A haven is where you spend the day. It determines your safety, secrecy, and comfort. Without one, you must constantly look for a new shelter.

options.full_description: You have no permanent place for your day's rest.
options.mechanic: Every morning you must find a new haven (usually requires a check).

HERD.name: Herd
HERD.description: A herd is a group of mortals whose blood you regularly use. These flaws mean you have trouble interacting with potential victims.

options.full_description: Your behavior frightens people on an instinctive level. You look like a dangerous predator, and people sense it.
options.mechanic:
−2d10 to hunting checks (unless violent methods are used).
−1d10 to social checks when trying to calm people down.
You cannot have a Herd.

FOLKLORIC.name: Folkloric Flaws
FOLKLORIC.description: Oddities and weaknesses rooted in ancient fears and the curse of the blood. These traits can heavily affect a vampire's behavior and survival.

options.full_description: You're especially vulnerable to stakes. What sends other vampires into torpor kills you outright.
options.mechanic: If a stake pierces your heart, you meet final death immediately instead of entering torpor.

options.full_description: You take serious damage from things that are normally harmless to vampires.
options.mechanic:
Choose one source:
— ultraviolet light (damage as from the sun)
— silver (damage as from a weapon; a touch inflicts 1 aggravated wound)
— holy water (damage as from fire)

options.full_description: Certain things cause you irrational fear or revulsion.
options.mechanic:
When confronted with the chosen object, you must back away or spend 1 Willpower point.
Each block is taken separately.
Examples: sacred symbols, running water, an uninvited threshold, white animals, garlic, hawthorn, uncounted seeds.

options.full_description: When extremely hungry, your body manifests mystical wounds.
options.mechanic: At Hunger 4, bleeding wounds appear on you. This draws attention and may impose penalties on checks (at the Storyteller's discretion).

MASK.name: Mask
MASK.description: A Mask is your cover identity among mortals. It lets you hide your vampiric nature and live in human society. Flaws here mean your identity is vulnerable or exposed.

options.full_description: Your data appears in intelligence databases as potentially dangerous. Any attentive observer might suspect your true nature.
options.mechanic: You're under constant suspicion. Any serious check or investigation could lead to exposure.

options.full_description: Your death is officially on record, and someone might know about it.
options.mechanic: Any records check shows you're supposed to be dead. People who know this will be shocked.

ANACHRONISM.name: Anachronism
ANACHRONISM.description: Only Ancillae or older vampires can take this flaw.

options.full_description: You either spent a long time in torpor, or for some other reason never managed to adapt to the modern world. You don't know how to use computers or smartphones. Your Technology skill is 0 and cannot be raised.
options.mechanic: The Storyteller may reduce the pool of any check involving modern technology by 1d10.

options.full_description: You can't or won't think in modern terms. You hold principles that are seriously outdated, such as: "the Pope's word is law," "a woman is a delicate flower," "the lower classes exist to serve their betters," or "raze the enemy's holdings to the ground." These principles support your Humanity, but many find them quite offensive.
options.mechanic: When your principles come up, the social check pool is reduced by 1d10 if the other party doesn't share those views.

LOOKS.name: Looks
LOOKS.description: Not every vampire looks like an actor from a popular TV series. Some are far more beautiful, and others noticeably less so. These modifiers only apply when the character can be seen. The Storyteller decides exactly how they apply in a social conflict.

options.full_description: Your appearance provokes strong disgust and repels people.
options.mechanic: Pools for all social checks that Looks could affect decrease by 2d10.

options.full_description: Your appearance is noticeably unattractive and complicates social interaction.
options.mechanic: Pools for all social checks that Looks could affect decrease by 1d10.

RESOURCES.name: Resources
RESOURCES.description: This is your economic status, capital, and access to resources. It reflects not just money, but also property, connections, and source of income.

options.full_description: You have no money, no property, and no stable income.
options.mechanic: You have no access to resources, housing, or equipment without outside help.

STATUS.name: Status
STATUS.description: Status reflects your reputation and standing among Kindred within a specific faction (such as the Camarilla or the Anarchs). Flaws here mean trouble with trust or open hostility.

options.full_description: One of the factions hates and despises you. You betrayed it, harmed it, or worked against it.
options.mechanic: Members of this faction actively work against you whenever possible.

options.full_description: One of the factions doesn't trust you. You broke a promise or acted in a questionable way.
options.mechanic: −2d10 to social checks when dealing with this faction, until you restore trust.

FAME.name: Fame
FAME.description: Being known can be dangerous. If your reputation is negative or tied to trouble, it complicates dealing with people and can draw the attention of enemies.

options.full_description: You're known for serious misdeeds or dangerous events: a Masquerade breach, a crime, or a conflict with authority.
options.mechanic: The difficulty of social checks where the opponent's reaction matters increases by 2. In extreme cases, people may try to arrest or destroy you.

options.full_description: You provoke a negative reaction, or are tied to trouble and debts.
options.mechanic: The difficulty of social checks increases by 1 when others react negatively.

options.full_description: You've done something terrible, but only a few know about it.
options.mechanic: The effect is similar to Notoriety, but only applies once it's exposed or the secret becomes relevant.

INFLUENCE.name: Influence
INFLUENCE.description: This is your ability to affect mortal society through connections, status, or pressure. Flaws here mean a poor reputation or active opponents.

options.full_description: A particular group or part of the city actively works against you and your allies.
options.mechanic: When trying to convince third parties to help you, the dice pool is reduced by 2d10. The Storyteller regularly brings your opponents into the story.

options.full_description: You have a bad reputation among people.
options.mechanic: Social check pools are reduced by 1d10 when dealing with most people (except allies and loyal contacts).

ENEMIES.name: Enemies
ENEMIES.description: Enemies are mortals or groups who actively work against you. They show up whenever it benefits the Storyteller, and cause trouble.

options.full_description: You have an opponent with limited capabilities.
options.mechanic: Effectiveness: gifted mortal. The enemy shows up regularly and causes trouble.

options.full_description: You have a serious opponent or group.
options.mechanic: Effectiveness: desperate mortal, or a group. The enemy actively intervenes in the story.

options.full_description: You're opposed by an extremely dangerous opponent.
options.mechanic: Very high effectiveness. The enemy poses a constant threat.

FEEDING.name: Feeding
FEEDING.description: This group covers various restrictions on a vampire's feeding. If a character has one of them, it doesn't mean they have the others.

options.full_description: You can only satisfy your Hunger by devouring human flesh and organs. You're especially drawn to blood-rich organs: the heart, liver, lungs, placenta, spleen. If the victim has a resonance, it can only be obtained by eating the heart.
options.mechanic: You cannot satisfy Hunger by ordinary blood drinking — only by eating organs. A resonance is only available by eating the heart.

options.full_description: You feed exclusively on animal blood.
options.mechanic: To drink human blood, you must spend 2 Willpower points. Ventrue cannot take this flaw.

options.full_description: You can only fully satisfy your Hunger with the blood of supernatural beings.
options.mechanic: When feeding on ordinary blood, you always retain at least 1 point of Hunger (or more, depending on Blood Potency).

options.full_description: You find it unacceptable to drink the blood of a certain category of people (for example: addicts, children, police officers, the innocent, etc.).
options.mechanic: If you break this rule, you gain Stains (as a deviation from the chronicle's theme). This can also be triggered if you watch others drink such blood.

SUBSTANCE_USE.name: Substance Use
SUBSTANCE_USE.description: Some vampires crave blood that contains a particular substance. They can feed on any blood, but suffer without the substance they need. Be sure to choose which substance matters to the character.

options.full_description: You're heavily dependent on a particular substance in the blood. Without it, you have a hard time functioning.
options.mechanic: If your last victim's blood didn't contain the needed substance, all checks not related to immediately obtaining the substance suffer a -2d10 penalty.

options.full_description: You prefer blood with a particular substance, and feel worse without it.
options.mechanic: If your last victim's blood didn't contain the needed substance, all checks not related to obtaining the substance suffer a -1d10 penalty.

BLOOD_BOND.name: Blood Bond
BLOOD_BOND.description: Blood bonds affect vampires differently. Some fall under their influence easily, others resist longer. These flaws strengthen your dependence on bonds.

options.full_description: You fall under someone else's power the moment you taste their vitae. For a bond to form on you, drinking another vampire's blood just once is enough, rather than the usual three times. You begin the game as your sire's vassal, or must explain why the bond was broken.
options.mechanic: Just 1 sip of vitae is enough to form a bond, instead of 3.

options.full_description: Bonds feel especially pleasant to you, and you resist their influence more poorly.
options.mechanic: The pool for any check made against your liege's will is reduced by 1d10.

options.full_description: Blood bonds last longer on you than on other vampires.
options.mechanic: The bond weakens more slowly: it takes 3 months to lower it by one step.

THIN_BLOODED.name: Thin-blooded Flaws
THIN_BLOODED.description: These flaws reflect the weakness of the blood and the unstable nature of thin-blooded vampires. Each flaw grants 1 dot and must be balanced by a merit.

options.full_description: Your fangs are undeveloped or useless.
options.mechanic: You cannot feed by biting — only through wounds or tools.

options.full_description: Your blood cannot sustain Disciplines without replenishment.
options.mechanic: If you don't drink vampire blood within a week, you lose access to all Disciplines. They return once you feed on vitae.

options.full_description: You inherit your sire's clan weakness.
options.mechanic: Choose a clan bane (level 1).

options.full_description: You're under constant pressure from the Camarilla.
options.mechanic: The Camarilla is hostile toward you and may pursue you whenever possible.

options.full_description: Your body is decaying and looks dead.
options.mechanic: −1d10 to social checks with mortals. You're medically classified as dead.

options.full_description: Your Beast is as dangerous as that of full vampires.
options.mechanic: You use the standard vampire frenzy rules.

options.full_description: The Anarchs have rejected you.
options.mechanic: You don't get the Anarchs' support. You cannot take "Friends Among the Anarchs."

options.full_description: You cannot use blood to heal.
options.mechanic: You heal like a mortal. You cannot take "Vampiric Resilience."

Autocrat.long:
The Autocrat seeks power for its own sake, not because he wants to make some progressive idea a reality. He might even believe he'd wield the power he gains better than those who currently hold it, but in the end that doesn't matter to him — he's only interested in power itself. Dictators, gang leaders, school bullies, corporate raiders, and the like fall under the Autocrat archetype. A Kindred Autocrat might crave recognition or strive to become the leader of his coterie.

Willpower regain: every time he gains power over other individuals.

Bon Vivant.long:
The Bon Vivant knows that life (and unlife) is empty and meaningless, and that the only way to fill it with meaning is to enjoy it to the fullest. A Bon Vivant isn't necessarily an irresponsible carouser. He might believe that business should always be mixed with pleasure. Most Bon Vivants don't have a particularly high self-control rating. Hedonists, sybarites, playboys, trust-fund kids, and the like fall under the Bon Vivant archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he truly manages to let loose and enjoy life. If the indulgence is especially great, he may regain more than one point of Willpower.

Survivor.long:
The Survivor always pushes forward to the very end, regardless of difficulties and seemingly insurmountable obstacles. His refusal to accept defeat can win victories in situations where many others would have given up long ago. The Survivor can't stand it when those around him meekly accept whatever fate hands them. Outcasts, homeless wanderers, downtrodden idealists, and other people who cling tightly to life no matter what fall under the Survivor archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to overcome danger through sheer persistence, or gives advice that helps someone else hold out against hardship.

Curmudgeon.long:
The Curmudgeon is a thoroughgoing cynic who sees the bad in everything and believes there's no room for fun in life (or unlife). The Curmudgeon is often also a fatalist and a pessimist, and on top of that has not the slightest respect for the people around him. For the Curmudgeon, the glass is at best half empty. Internet trolls, pop-culture fans, members of Generation X, and the like fall under the Curmudgeon archetype.

Willpower regain: every time someone confirms his worst expectations. The Curmudgeon has to voice those expectations in advance — for example: "Just watch, the Prince is definitely going to screw us over."

Rebel.short: You don't follow other people's rules.
Rebel.long:
The Rebel is a disgruntled element opposing the established status quo and the current system. He hates the very idea of social hierarchy and will do everything he can to overthrow it, or at least challenge it. Among Rebels there are both those who genuinely believe in their ideas and those who simply hold a grudge against authority figures. Teenagers, insurgents, and nonconformists of every stripe fall under the Rebel archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he does something directly related to fighting the power he has chosen. When you pick this archetype, you need to specify which embodiment of authority the character opposes.

Visionary.long:
The Visionary is able to look past the mundane and glimpse something truly remarkable. Visionaries challenge social norms and reach for things others don't even dare imagine. Society as a whole generally doesn't view Visionaries very favorably, even though they're among those who bring progress and change. Philosophers, inventors, inspired creators, and the like fall under the Visionary archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to recruit new supporters to his idea. What matters is support for the idea itself, not whether it's actually realized.

Bravo.long:
The Bravo is a big, tough bully who gets a perverse pleasure out of tormenting the weak. In the Bravo's view, whoever is stronger is right. Strength is all that matters. Muggers, chauvinists, thugs, and the like fall under the Bravo archetype. A Kindred Bravo might abuse the power he holds, or eliminate anyone standing between him and his goal.

Willpower regain: every time he gets his way through brute force, threats, or psychological or social pressure.

Guru.short: Others come to you for spiritual guidance.
Guru.long:
The Guru's aura of enlightenment draws people to him. A Guru might be an ideologue of one of the Paths of Enlightenment, or simply a philosophizing thinker. Either way, the Guru inspires others toward spiritual and ideological accomplishments. People see the Guru as a calm, focused, and inspired figure. Cult leaders, Zen masters, Sabbat pack chaplains, and the like fall under the Guru archetype.

Willpower regain: every time his advice spurs someone toward a good deed they wouldn't otherwise have done, and every time he experiences a revelation related to his philosophy.

Deviant.short: The status quo is for sheep.
Deviant.long:
The Deviant is a social outcast, the bearer of extremely radical, socially unacceptable tastes, leanings, and beliefs. Deviants are thinkers and doers who simply don't fit the established status quo. Deviants feel that the world doesn't accept them, so they have no choice but to defy the norms of the traditional morality that rejects them. Extremists, eccentric celebrities, and town lunatics fall under the Deviant archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to flout the norms of public morality without consequence.

Enigma.short: Just when people think they're starting to understand you, you change the rules of the game.
Enigma.long:
The Enigma is a character who does strange things whose meaning only he understands. These inexplicable quirks might be a side effect of his Embrace, or simply the most familiar and effective way he has of achieving his goals. Everyone else considers him eccentric at best and insane at worst. Conspiracy theorists, undercover agents, and fanatics of the Jyhad fall under the Enigma archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to throw someone off balance and thereby successfully carry out his plan.

Idealist.long:
The Idealist believes — truly, blindly, and deeply — in some higher purpose or set of ethical principles. The object of the Idealist's faith can be fairly practical and concrete (like faith in the inevitable triumph of the Camarilla), or quite abstract. Idealists often have trouble reconciling their beliefs with the harsh realities of vampiric existence, and in their effort to stay true to their creed, they sometimes act against their own interests.

Willpower regain: every time he achieves a goal without compromising his principles, or performs a significant act driven purely by loyalty to his beliefs.

Analyst.short: The world around you is a riddle waiting to be solved.
Analyst.long:
To the Analyst, existence is a puzzle he can try to solve. The Analyst methodically examines every situation and every possibility, trying to logically predict what choice to make and what consequences to expect. This archetype is marked by a habit of thoroughly — and critically — studying everything around him. His system for evaluating reality may well be implausible and laughable, but that's unlikely to be an obstacle for its devoted adherent.

Willpower regain: every time a logical, systematic approach solves a problem, and every time information gathered earlier proves useful later on.

Capitalist.short: Why give it away when you can sell it?
Capitalist.long:
The Capitalist knows that demand creates supply, and that everything in the world has its price. On top of that, he understands perfectly well how to make Kindred and mortals want whatever he's offering. Appearance and social influence play an important role in the Capitalist's life, but he never forgets that any little thing can be turned into an advantage. Merchants, mercenaries, flatterers, and the like fall under the Capitalist archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he pulls off a profitable deal. The subject of the deal can be anything at all — from a used car to information, someone else's debts, and so on.

Penitent.short: You are cursed, and that curse must be atoned for.
Penitent.long:
Penitents typically suffer from either low self-esteem or past psychological trauma. A character like this feels obligated to atone for his guilt. The Penitent's motivation doesn't have to be religious; some of them simply want to make up, somehow, for the evil they've done or are still forced to do. Self-flagellators, criminals who've broken with their past, and the like fall under this archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to atone for at least one of his misdeeds. The act of atonement must be equal in weight to the misdeed.

Conformist.long:
The Conformist is a loyal follower, ready to carry out orders and follow a leader. He'd rather not take responsibility for others, preferring instead to go along with the majority and help his comrades as best he can. The Conformist is typically drawn to the brightest, strongest individual around, whom he's immediately ready to obey. Star-struck fans, rank-and-file political party members, and the masses in general fall under the Conformist archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he helps the group he belongs to, or the leader whose orders he follows, achieve success.

Masochist.short: Pain reminds you that you still exist.
Masochist.long:
The Masochist lives to find out what he's capable of — to learn just how much pain he can't bear. He takes pleasure in being humiliated, insulted, and subjected to emotional or even physical pain. For the Masochist, suffering is a means of self-identification. Urban savages, especially fanatical athletes, people with clinical depression, and the like fall under the Masochist archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he achieves success through suffering. He also regains two points of Willpower every time he discovers a new, previously unknown way to feel pain.

Conniver.short: Other people exist to be used for profit.
Conniver.long:
Why do something yourself when you can arrange for someone else to do it? The Conniver always tries to find the easiest, fastest, and most carefree path to success. The Conniver himself is genuinely convinced that anyone in his place would do the same thing, and that people only get angry because he did it first. Con artists, traveling salesmen, street urchins, entrepreneurs, and the like fall under the Conniver archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to gain something through deception, or convinces a victim to help him while disregarding their own interests.

Martyr.short: You suffer in the name of a higher cause.
Martyr.long:
The Martyr endures hardship for the sake of his ideals, consoling himself with the thought that his suffering eases someone else's burden. Some Martyrs suffer ostentatiously, demanding attention and sympathy from those around them, but others among them have a sincere and unshakeable faith. Revolutionaries, idealistic utopians, rejected sufferers, and the like fall under the Martyr archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he takes harm or suffers a serious loss in the name of his ideals or someone else's wellbeing.

Show-Off.short: Oh no, you're not the star of the show — you ARE the show!
Show-Off.long:
Show-Offs are flashy, vain individuals who crave everyone's attention and always strive to be the brightest of all the stars. The Show-Off needs other people only as a crowd of enthusiastic fans. Attention is the Show-Off's main motivator. Nothing excites a Show-Off more than a new audience he can show off in front of in all his glory. Performers, spoiled children, and other individuals with low self-esteem fall under the Show-Off archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to impress someone.

Pedagogue.short: Your knowledge saves the lives of those around you.
Pedagogue.long:
The Pedagogue knows something, and — more importantly — wants to share that knowledge with others. Whether it's a sense of calling or simply a desire to help people, the Pedagogue will do everything he can to make sure his message is heard and understood. Pedagogues range from teachers truly worthy of the title to windbags in love with the sound of their own voice. Instructors, professors, and all sorts of masters of their craft fall under the Pedagogue archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he learns of the successes of his "students" — those with whom he's shared his wisdom and experience.

Dilettante.long:
The Dilettante is interested in everything but focused on nothing. He quickly loses interest in things, never finishes anything, and constantly flits from idea to idea. The Dilettante's enthusiasm often draws others in, but sooner or later they're left on their own when he suddenly drops everything and moves on to his next fascination. Kindred Dilettantes are most often Toreador, especially those known as "Poseurs."

Willpower regain: every time he gets excited about something new and abandons his previous interest.

Loner.long:
The Loner is so different that he stands out even in a crowd. People see Loners as pariahs, cold and withdrawn individuals, but the truth is that, given the choice, a Loner would rather be left alone with himself. Criminals, radicals, and freethinkers of every stripe fall under the Loner archetype. Kindred Loners are most often members of scattered clans like the Gangrel or Ravnos.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to accomplish something useful for the whole coterie on his own.

Eye of the Storm.short: No one can tie you to the chaos raging around you.
Eye of the Storm.long:
This character spreads chaos around him while maintaining a calm, unruffled appearance. Disorder, madness, death, and destruction circle him like stormbirds. To him, unlife is an endless, complicated game of elimination with uncertain odds of success. Gang leaders, certain politicians, and other influential individuals fall under the Eye of the Storm archetype.

Willpower regain: every time civil unrest, or some other event that's less catastrophic but just as chaotic, happens nearby.

Caregiver.short: Everyone needs someone to look after them.
Caregiver.long:
Everyone needs a shoulder to cry on. The Caregiver is one of those willing to offer comfort, and people often come to him to talk about their problems. Nurses, doctors, psychiatrists, and the like fall under the Caregiver archetype. Kindred Caregivers often try to protect the mortals whose blood they drink, or create traumatic situations themselves so they can comfort those who go through them.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to protect or comfort someone.

Director.short: You need to make sure everything goes according to plan.
Director.long:
For the Director, nothing is worse than chaos and disorder. Directors aren't interested in power for its own sake, but in the process itself of optimizing how groups function and building structures capable of efficiently carrying out the tasks set before them. Coaches, teachers, and many politicians fall under the Director archetype. Kindred Directors can be either supporters of upholding existing laws or advocates for installing new, more effective leaders.

Willpower regain: every time an individual or group he's helped with deeds or advice achieves success or pulls off a very difficult task.

Perfectionist.short: You strive for an unattainable ideal.
Perfectionist.long:
The Perfectionist strives to make everything he does meet the highest standards of quality. Work done half-heartedly brings the Perfectionist no satisfaction whatsoever. The Perfectionist only demands of others what he demands of himself. Opera divas, artists, architects, and the like fall under the Perfectionist archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he achieves a goal without a single flaw or shortcoming, and every time his flawless example inspires another character to an equally successful accomplishment.

Competitor.short: You must be first at everything.
Competitor.long:
The Competitor takes pleasure in striving for victory and winning it. For the Competitor, every task is a challenge to overcome, or a contest to win. The Competitor sees almost every form of interaction with the world around him as a chance to show himself at his best. Corporate raiders, professional athletes, passionate researchers, and the like fall under the Competitor archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he successfully overcomes a challenge he's taken on, or gets through a large-scale, dramatic trial. For an especially difficult trial, he may regain more than one point of Willpower.

Zealot.short: You're ready to labor for a higher cause.
Zealot.long:
The Zealot takes pleasure in doing what he genuinely loves. Whether it's the fearsome joy of battle, religious ecstasy, or the pleasure of reading a good book, his favorite pursuit gives the Zealot strength and lets him weather any blow fate deals him. Unlike the Fanatic, the Zealot is driven not by a sense of duty but by sincere enthusiasm. When you choose this archetype, you need to specify exactly what the character is so passionate about.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to lose himself in his favorite pursuit, or draws someone else into it. The character may lose a temporary point of Willpower if he goes a long time without being able to pursue it.

Child.short: Someone who needs to be taken care of.
Child.long:
The Child is an individual who still hasn't fully formed, either emotionally or as a person. Although the Child is quite capable of taking care of himself, he'd rather always have someone nearby to look after him and indulge all his whims and desires. Actual children, spoiled rich kids, certain addicts, and the like fall under the Child archetype. A Kindred Child doesn't necessarily have to have been literally Embraced as a child.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to get someone's help or support for free.

Sadist.short: You live to inflict pain on others.
Sadist.long:
The Sadist lives to cause pain and suffering to others. Killing is too easy for him. Torture is the only way to truly break someone. The Sadist is constantly searching for new and ever more painful ways to push his victims to the limits of their mental and physical endurance. Drill instructors, vengeful lovers, cruel madmen, and many others fall under the Sadist archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he causes someone pain purely for his own enjoyment.

Soldier.short: The order must be carried out, but only you decide exactly how.
Soldier.long:
The Soldier isn't a meek order-follower. He does carry out orders, of course, but he's by no means obligated to obey them without question. The Soldier is more independent than the Conformist, but too tied to the idea of hierarchy to be a Loner. He brings creativity to achieving the goals others set for him, but only when the order comes from someone with the right to give it.

Willpower regain: every time he successfully carries out an order he's been given. The more difficult the task, the deeper the satisfaction. An especially impressive success may let him regain more than one point of Willpower.

Thrill-Seeker.short: Risk is what gives eternity meaning.
Thrill-Seeker.long:
The Thrill-Seeker lives in search of danger. Unlike those boring types who take a more sensible approach to life, the Thrill-Seeker actively seeks out dangerous situations himself. The Thrill-Seeker isn't suicidal and isn't looking to self-destruct; all he's after is the rush of sensation. Extreme sports enthusiasts, petty criminals, exhibitionists, and the like fall under the Thrill-Seeker archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he successfully pulls off a risky undertaking he's chosen to take on. Risky doesn't mean reckless: the Storyteller need not reward mindlessly putting himself in danger.

Sociopath.long:
Inferior beings, whether living or undead, disrupt the harmony of all existence, and therefore deserve to die. The Sociopath might not see killing as anything reprehensible at all, but rather view it as a worthy act that benefits society. Whatever argument you raise, the Sociopath has a populist comeback ready, along the lines of "Darwin would approve" and "survival of the fittest."

Willpower regain: if, after a major altercation, it turns out he personally contributed the most to the overall body count. This also applies to cases where he was the only one doing the killing.

Judge.short: Your fair judgment will make the world better.
Judge.long:
The Judge is devoted to rationalism and logical thinking, and champions fairness as the most effective model for resolving conflicts. Despite his drive to solve problems, the Judge rarely shows any inclination toward visionary thinking, preferring instead to use proven models and rely on solid facts. Engineers, lawyers, doctors, and the like fall under the Judge archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to solve a problem by analyzing the available evidence, or peacefully settles a dispute between conflicting groups.

Architect.short: You create something that will endure.
Architect.long:
The Architect is driven by the pursuit of a great purpose. He feels truly happy only when he's creating something eternal, something of lasting value. People in every age have needed some material embodiment of great ideas, and Architects do everything they can to bring those aspirations to life. Inventors, pioneers, city planners, entrepreneurs, and the like fall under the Architect archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he creates something significant or lasting.

Traditionalist.short: As it was before, so it shall remain.
Traditionalist.long:
The Traditionalist holds to familiar, time-tested views and relies only on reliable methods, long since proven, for achieving his goals. The Traditionalist considers the status quo an acceptable, even preferable, state of affairs, especially when the alternative is change with unclear and unpredictable consequences. Conservatives, judges, figures of authority, and the like fall under the Traditionalist archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to prove that the old ways are better than the new, and every time a conservative position he voiced earlier turns out to be the best solution to a pressing problem.

Trickster.long:
The Trickster sees the absurd in everything. No matter how bleak life (or unlife) may seem, the Trickster can always find a reason to laugh about it. Some Tricksters even display devotion to lofty ideals, challenging social dogmas by exposing and mocking their flaws. Comedians, satirists, social critics, and the like fall under the Trickster archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to lift the spirits of those around him, especially if doing so also eases his own pain.

Fanatic.long:
The Fanatic has a cause, and he lays his own life (or unlife) on its altar. The Fanatic is so devoted to it that he's tormented by guilt whenever he does something unrelated to achieving his higher goal. As far as this character is concerned, the end justifies any means. When you choose this archetype, you need to name exactly what the character is so single-mindedly devoted to.

Willpower regain: every time he carries out some task directly and immediately related to his chosen higher goal.

Freak.short: The disgust on other people's faces is the best reason to celebrate.
Freak.long:
The Freak strives to shock and scandalize the people around him with inexplicable acts and inappropriate behavior. For the Freak himself, this is just a way to stand out from the crowd and manipulate others. And although the people around him may see the Freak as the Devil incarnate, that suits the Freak just fine. Shock rockers, rebellious teenagers, circus sideshow performers, people with Munchausen syndrome, and the like fall under the Freak archetype.

Willpower regain: every time his actions provoke fear or disgust.

Chameleon.short: You can blend into any environment.
Chameleon.long:
The independent, self-sufficient Chameleon carefully studies the behavior and customs of everyone he has to deal with. Armed with this knowledge, he's able to pass himself off as a member of any social group or stratum he's familiar with. The Chameleon is so good at this that when he puts on someone else's persona, even his own sire might not recognize him. Spies, con artists, impostors, and drag performers fall under the Chameleon archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he manages to pass himself off as someone else and thereby achieve something useful for himself or his coterie.

Monster.short: You are cursed, and that curse must be accepted!
Monster.long:
The Monster knows he's a monster, and acts accordingly. Evil and suffering are his tools. If evil can be done, the Monster will do it. This character doesn't commit evil for pleasure, but in order to more deeply understand his own monstrous nature. Psychologically unstable sociopaths, deeply traumatized individuals, and the like fall under the Monster archetype.

Willpower regain: when choosing this archetype, you need to specify the type of misdeed most meaningful to the character — committing that chosen misdeed lets the Monster regain a point of Willpower. The Storyteller should be extremely selective about letting Monsters regain Willpower this way.

Egotist.short: Nothing and no one matters but you.
Egotist.long:
As far as the Egotist is concerned, the only thing that matters is himself. Everyone has the right to claim as their own only what they're able to hold on to. The Egotist himself isn't necessarily a criminal or a scoundrel. He's simply self-sufficient, refuses to live up to other people's expectations, and always looks out for his own interests. Pimps, capitalists, con artists, and the like fall under the Egotist archetype.

Willpower regain: every time he gains a moral or material benefit through his selfish behavior. He regains two points of Willpower if he also manages to hide his weaknesses from an opponent in the process. He also regains a point of Willpower whenever his group succeeds at the expense of a rival group.
